EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a cold frontal passage the evening of December 11th, a period of west-southwesterly winds directed moderate to heavy lake effect snow in Erie County from early in the morning December 12th through the afternoon hours. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 MPH led to blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility, with some accidents reported, including along PA Route 5. A peak snow amount of 9 inches was reported by a trained spotter in North East.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following a cold frontal passage the evening of December 11th, a period of west-southwesterly winds directed moderate to heavy lake effect snow in Erie County from early in the morning December 12th through the afternoon hours. Wind gusts of 30 to 40 MPH led to blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility. Some accidents were reported, including a 4 car accident resulting in one injury along PA Route 5 in Lake City. A peak snow amount of 9 inches was reported by a trained spotter in North East, with a spotter in Harborcreek measuring 7.4 inches. Erie International Airport reported 3.9 inches of snow with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold southwesterly winds shifted westerly during the daytime hours on January 16th, briefly shifting an intense lake effect snow band impacting western New York south into northern Erie County, Pennsylvania. This event was short-lived with snow shifting north and back into New York by early that evening. However, snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred for a few hours under the heart of the lake effect band across northern portions of Erie County. The heaviest snow amount reported was 7.2 inches in North East township by a trained spotter. Other selected spotter reports include 6.5 inches in Greene Township, 6.1 inches in the city of Erie and 6 inches in Lake City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold southwesterly winds shifted westerly during the daytime hours on January 16th, briefly shifting an intense lake effect snow band impacting western New York south into northern Erie County, Pennsylvania. This event was short-lived with snow shifting north and back into New York by early that evening. However, snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred for a few hours under the heart of the lake effect band across northern portions of Erie County. Selected reports from trained spotters include 7 inches in the borough of North East, 6.1 inches in the city of Erie and 6 inches in Lake City. Visibilities below one-quarter of a mile and snow covered roads led to hazardous travel conditions and accidents during the heaviest snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure tracked out of the Mississippi Valley and into the Great Lakes on January 9th. Strong south to southeast winds occurred ahead of this low pressure on January 9th, causing localized damaging winds mainly along and north of I-90 near Lake Erie.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shortly before 11:00 AM, winds caused roof damage to a building along East 12th street in Erie, closing the road for several hours. At around the same time, multiple large trees were uprooted near the intersection of Cabot Avenue and Halley Street in Lawrence Park. During the afternoon, a large tree near downtown Erie was uprooted and crushed a parked truck. At 1:48 PM, Erie Airport measured a peak wind gust of 63 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure lifted out of the lower Mississippi Valley and into the Great Lakes January 12th and 13th. Strong southeasterly winds ahead of the low caused damage in northern Erie County during the evening of January 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 7:06 PM, Erie International Airport measured a 69 MPH wind gust. These wind gusts caused downed trees and power-lines across the county with over 3000 customers losing power.

This event was short-lived with snow shifting north and back into New York by early that evening. The heavy snow was confined to locations near and north of I-90. However, snow rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred for a few hours under the heart of the lake effect band across northern portions of Erie County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak clipper moved across the Ohio Valley on January 19th bringing light snow to the region. As the clipper progressed east into the 20th it intensified off the East Coast as cold air poured into the region. Generally light snow with the clipper on January 19th was enhanced by moisture picked up off of Lake Erie as colder air poured in. Continued cold air advection behind the East Coast low into the 20th and early 21st allowed lake effect snow showers to continue, particularly in the higher elevations in southern Erie County and northern Crawford County. There were two periods in which snowfall was steadiest and at times heavy: During the afternoon and early evening hours on January 19th, especially across Erie County, and then from late-evening on the 19th through the evening on the 20th in locally intense lake effect bands, especially across southern Erie County and northern Crawford County. Snow rates reached 1 inch per hour at times with visibility occasionally falling below a quarter of a mile. Wind gusts up to 30 miles per hour led to patchy blowing snow, especially on the 20th. A trained spotter reported a prolonged storm total of 19 inches in Greenfield Township. Other select spotter reports include 16 inches in Edinboro, 15.6 inches in Venango, 13.5 inches in Meadville and 10.5 inches in North East.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak clipper moved across the Ohio Valley on January 19th bringing light snow to the region. As the clipper progressed east into the 20th it intensified off the East Coast as cold air poured into the region. The light snow with the clipper on January 19th was enhanced by moisture picked up off of Lake Erie as colder air poured in. Continued cold air advection behind the East Coast low into the 20th and early 21st allowed lake effect snow showers to continue, though this activity was generally lighter north of I-90 and close to Lake Erie. The snow was steadiest and at times heavy during the afternoon and early evening hours on January 19th. Snow rates reached 1 inch per hour at times with visibility occasionally falling below one-half of a mile. Wind gusts up to 30 miles per hour led to patchy blowing snow, especially on the 20th. A trained spotter reported a prolonged storm total of 10.5 inches in North East, mostly falling on January 19th. Another spotter reported 9.6 inches in Lake City. A CoCoRaHS observer in Erie reported 8.3 inches. Erie International Airport measured 6.7 inches of snow with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwesterly winds on the backside of departing low pressure produced gusts to 60+ mph across portions of the southern Allegheny Mountains of Pennsylvania during the morning and afternoon hours of January 10, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust estimated near 80 mph knocked over a barn and overturned a vehicle inside of the barn along Piney Creek Road near Williamsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving warm front brought dense fog to eastern Pennsylvania during the midday and afternoon hours on January 24th. As the front continued to lift north, the fog slowly dissipated during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with visibility 1/4 mile or less was reported for several hours at Wings Field Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure moving northeastward across the Great Lakes brought strong southeasterly winds to the region. Winds peaked during the evening of January 9th. Widespread reports of scattered tree damage were received from across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A DEOS station in White Clay Creek/West Grove reported a peak gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the region with steady southerly flow pushing water up into coastal communities both along the ocean and within the Delaware River/Bay and Chesapeake Bay. Water levels on the tidal Delaware River were higher due to 2 to 4+ inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate and major flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries with both high tides on January 10th. There were many road closures with water entering vulnerable homes and businesses. The tide gauge at Bridesburg reached a record level of 10.99 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at the Philadelphia Coast Guard Station reached a record level of 10.66 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary just south of the region resulted in stagnant, moist air across the area. Warm air lifted over the boundary facilitated the development of dense fog late on the 26th. The fog dissipated early on the 27th as the front shifted south and drier air advected into the area from the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog with visibility 1/4 mile or less was observed for several hours at Reading Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked to the northwest of the forecast area on Tuesday into early Wednesday (January 9-10th). A cold front associated with the low swept through the region on Wednesday. In the system���s wake, total rainfall amounts varied from about 1.50 inches up to about 4.00 inches. The rain fell on already saturated soils and there was some snow on the ground that melted and also contributed to the runoff.  As a result, river flooding was prevalent across the Passaic Basin, Raritan Basin, and southeast Pennsylvania basins including the Schuylkill and Brandywine. The Brandywine flooding extended in to Delaware. Smaller streams and rivers across northern New Castle County in Delaware, such as the Christina, Red Clay, and White Clay also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across southeast Pennsylvania and northern Delaware, flooding occurred on the Brandywine River, the Schuylkill River, the Christina River, Red Clay Creek, and White Clay Creek.  The Cooch���s Bridge gauge crested at 12.52 ft. The Langhorne gauge crested at 13.91 ft. The Downingtown gauge crested at 11.41 ft. The Newark gauge crested at 15.14 ft. The Chadd���s Ford gauge crested at 12.84 ft. The Wooddale gauge crested at 9.03 ft. The Graterford gauge crested at 16.30 ft. The Chester gauge crested at 12.55 ft. The Philadelphia gauge crested at 12.13 ft. The Pottstown gauge crested at 13.65 ft. The Norristown gauge crested at 16.29 ft. The Wilmington gauge crested at 17.39 ft. All of these crests are above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By November, the dry conditions apparent across the upper Ohio Valley resulted in expansion of Extreme Drought (D3) conditions to portions of southwest Pennsylvania. This was short lived as rainfall did bring those regions back to Moderate Drought (D1) conditions by the beginning of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme Drought (D3) wash expanded to portions of the county at the beginning of November with improvement back to Severe Drought (D2) conditions November 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation in the form of a wintry mix of rain changing to sleet, snow, and freezing rain occurred across the Pocono Plateau on the morning of the 28th. In the higher elevations the Poconos, temperatures near freezing resulted in some light ice accretion in addition to some light snow and sleet accumulations near 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation in the form of a wintry mix of rain changing to sleet, snow, and freezing rain occurred across the Pocono Plateau on the morning of the 28th. In the higher elevations the Poconos, temperatures near freezing resulted in some light ice accretion in addition to some light snow and sleet accumulations near 1. The Mount Pocono ASOS reported a flat ice accretion of 0.04. A nearby CoCoRaHS observer noted that the ice was confined mainly to the trees, but some patchy ice occurred on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread light to moderate snowfall accumulations across the region. The heaviest snowfall occurred near the Delaware Valley in the Philadelphia metro area with totals around 3 to 5. A coating of snow also occurred farther north in the Lehigh Valley and southern Poconos with amounts around 1 to 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 4 to 5 across much of the county. The highest snowfall report was from the Belmont area with 5.9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep upper level low pressure moved into the western Great Lakes on November 20th. A strong shortwave rotating around this upper low across Ohio helped to drive a cold front across the Upper Ohio Valley during the late afternoon hours. A thin line of showers accompanied the front, with little to no lightning. The front worked with sufficient instability aloft to bring strong wind gusts to the surface, with several in the 45 to 60 MPH range according to various observations. These gusts were responsible for the many reports of wind damage received across southwest Pennsylvania. Strong low-level shear and weakly veered wind profiles also supported a brief tornado on the north side of Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large Cleveland Pear tree was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level low pressure center associated with severe weather on the 20th continued to track across the Upper Ohio Valley on the 21st and slowly off to the east thereafter. Associated surface low pressure tracked from Michigan towards southern New England over the same period. This low drove a cold front across the region, changing lingering rain over to snow. While lowland locations received minor accumulation at most from this system, colder temperatures and upslope flow was able to produce more significant accumulation in the higher elevations, lasting into the 23rd.||For the Laurel Highlands, storm totals of 4 to 9 inches were reported.  The highest totals of around 9 inches were received from the Ohiopyle and Champion areas of Fayette County, with a report of 8 inches at New Florence in Westmoreland County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported storm totals included 15 inches near Champion and 8 inches at New Florence.

The front worked with sufficient instability aloft to bring strong wind gusts to the surface, with several in the 45 to 60 MPH range according to various observations. These gusts were responsible for the many reports of wind damage received across southwest Pennsylvania. Strong low-level shear and weakly veered wind profiles also supported a brief tornado on the north side of Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Dutchtown Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow squalls accompanied a brief shot of arctic air following a cold frontal passage on March 10, 2024. Some of these squalls produced visibilities below a quarter of a mile and briefly made roads slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. An RWIS site on US Route 22 near Cresson reported that road surface temperatures dropped below freezing, the level of grip dropped below 0.5, and visibility dropped below one quarter mile in a snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm advection ahead of low pressure pushing up the Ohio Valley produced a 4 to 6 hour period of moderate to heavy snow across the southern Allegheny Mountains of Pennsylvania during the morning of January 9, 2024. As much as 12 inches of snowfall was observed atop the Allegheny Front in eastern Somerset and eastern Cambria counties, with localized snowfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour. This system also produced 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain across the Cumberland and Susquehanna valleys, resulting in localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 7 to 9 inches of snowfall across higher elevations of Cambria County as snow fell throughout the morning hours of January 9, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration light synoptic snowfall followed by upslope/lake-effect snow showers produced 6 to 10 inches of snow across most of Somerset County from January 19-20, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 6 to 10 inches of snowfall across all but far southeastern and far southwestern Somerset County from January 19 to January 20, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure stationed off the New England Coast allowed for a long duration snowfall across much of central Pennsylvania beginning in the morning hours of January 6th with the bulk of snowfall ending in the evening hours. As the low pressure system moved further east on January 7th, northwesterly flow allowed for orographically enhanced snow showers to continue into January 7th along the Allegheny Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 4 to 6 inches of snow across the higher elevations of Adams County as snow began in the morning hours of January 6, 2024 and continued through the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold advection behind a cold front brought in much colder air on March 10th.  Steep lapse rates and marginal instability led to the development of snow showers and a few snow squalls that morning.  More numerous snow showers continued into the overnight hours, resulting in some accumulations, highest north of I-80 and along the ridges.  Also, the steep lapse rates, strong surface pressure gradient, and strong pressure rises behind the front led to a period of strong wind gusts, topping out in the 45 to 58 MPH range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Visibility dropped below 0.25 mile in a heavy snow shower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across north-central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of March 20, 2024 bringing some light precipitation before a cold front moved across all of central Pennsylvania later in the day producing some weaker snow squalls throughout the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A RWIS site on Interstate 80 at Penfield recorded rapid visibility drops below a quarter of a mile with heavy snow observed on webcam. Road surface temperatures dropped 7 degrees in 20 minutes but stayed above freezing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly winds behind a deepening low pressure system off the East Coast occasionally gusted to 35-45 mph across central and eastern Pennsylvania. There was one report of trees on wires in southern Clinton County as a result of the winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust estimated near 45 mph knocked down trees onto wires onto Route 477 in Greene Township, Clinton County between West Winter Road and Turkey Hollow Road, closing the road in both directions for more than two hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a cold front, deep mixing and a strong low-level jet contributed to gusty winds on March 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gusts of up to 49 MPH were measured at Latrobe Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gusts of up to 46 MPH were measured at Pittsburgh International Airport.

An RWIS site near Snow Shoe recorded a rapid visibility drop with the road surface temperatures dropping to 33.4F at this site, but dropped below freezing at the site just across the Centre/Clearfield county boundary. Roads were slushy at the site across the county line and remained wet due to chemical treatment at this RWIS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow squalls accompanied a brief shot of arctic air following a cold frontal passage on March 10, 2024. Some of these squalls produced visibilities below a quarter of a mile and briefly made roads slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. An RWIS site on Interstate 80 near Penfield reported that the road surface temperature quickly dropped below freezing, the level of grip dropped below 0.30, and visibility dropped below one quarter mile due to heavy snowfall associated with the squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow squalls accompanied a brief shot of arctic air following a cold frontal passage on March 10, 2024. Some of these squalls produced visibilities below a quarter of a mile and briefly made roads slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A series of webcams on US Route 22 near Cresson and near Gallitzin showed visibility less than a quarter of a mile with heavy snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts (45-55 mph) following a cold frontal passage on March 10, 2024 produced isolated reports of trees down across central Pennsylvania, including a tree that fell onto a home in Cumberland County and caused a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds estimated near 50 mph knocked down trees and wires onto Route 441 between Falmouth Road and Turnpike Road in Conoy Township, closing Route 441 for several hours for clean up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved through, bringing strong onshore flow, which resulted in water piling up along the coast and within back bays and tributaries. Moderate coastal flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries. There were many road closures with water entering vulnerable homes and businesses. The tide gauge at the Philadelphia Coast Guard Station reached a level of 9.45 feet MLLW and the tide gauge at Marcus Hook reached a level of 8.70 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to earlier clouds and rain, low-level instability was muted during the evening of March 14th.  However, an approaching cold front was able to tap into elevated instability and work with sufficient shear to create another round of strong thunderstorms. While a damaging wind threat was negated by the stable low levels, several reports of large hail were received in far western Pennsylvania.

Northwest PA resided in the warm sector from 7 AM on the 27th through 7 AM EST on the 28th. Simultaneously, a surface low moved east-northeastward from southern MN to near Georgian Bay and allowed the trailing cold front to sweep eastward to western OH. During the early morning of the 28th, a cluster to multicell thunderstorms associated with a low-level jet undergoing moist isentropic ascent persisted generally eastward across northwest PA and produced hail up to quarter-size in Cambridge Springs. These storms occurred amidst moderate effective bulk shear, mid level lapse rates near 7C/km, and weak MUCAPE.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-size in Cambridge Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure to the northeast resulted in spotty high wind gusts across portions of eastern Pennsylvania during the morning hours of March 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph gust was observed at Pocono Mountains Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing coastal low pressure resulted in spotty high winds across parts of eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of March 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph gust was observed at Pocono Mountains Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to the hail event earlier in the month, low-level instability was muted during the evening of March 30th.  After rain earlier in the day, another round of thunderstorms developed along an advancing cold front in central Ohio and rolled to the south and east.  Strong shear and elevated instability led to a couple reports of marginally large hail.

A coastal storm moved through, bringing strong onshore flow, which resulted in water piling up along the coast and within back bays and tributaries. Moderate coastal flooding was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread roadway flooding occurred in the tidal communities of lower Bucks County. The tide gauge at Burlington (Burlington County, New Jersey) reached 10.58 feet MLLW, and the tide gauge at Newbold reached 11.50 feet MLLW.

The tide gauge at Bridesburg reached a level of 9.82 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at the Philadelphia Coast Guard Station reached a level of 9.45 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure deepened over the New England states, cold northwest flow, wrap around moisture, a series of surface troughs and lake enhancement led to accumulating snow across Northwest Pennsylvania. Locally heavy snow occurred, mainly focused on the higher terrain across southern Erie County. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 11 inches of snow in Corry. Elsewhere, spotters reported 9.7 inches in Union City with 6 inches reported in several other communities. Wind gusts to 40 MPH led to some blowing and drifting snow with visibility falling to under one-quarter of a mile at times due to a combination of falling snow and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As low pressure deepened over the New England states, cold northwest flow, wrap around moisture, a series of surface troughs and lake enhancement led to accumulating snow. Portions of southern Erie County received heavy snow from this event. A CoCoRaHS observer measured 11 inches of snow in Corry. Elsewhere, spotters reported 9.7 inches in Union City with 6 inches reported in Greenfield Township, Edinboro, and Greene Township. Wind gusts to 40 MPH led to some blowing and drifting snow with visibility falling to under one-quarter of a mile at times due to a combination of falling snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across north-central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of March 20, 2024 bringing some light precipitation before a cold front moved across all of central Pennsylvania later in the day producing some weaker snow squalls throughout the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Previous snowfall melted on the road and temperatures dipped below freezing during the early morning hours of March 20, 2024. This led to freezing on bridges and overpasses in Tioga County, where a two-vehicle accident occurred closing State Route 49 in Westfield Township for approximately two hours. There were three injuries reported due to the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure to the northeast coupled with some trailing showers and dry air resulted in spotty high wind gusts across portions of Philadelphia during the late afternoon and early evening of March 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Philadelphia International Airport reported a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak clipper moved across the Ohio Valley on January 19th bringing light snow to the region. As the clipper progressed east into the 20th it intensified off the East Coast as cold air poured into the region. Generally light snow with the clipper on January 19th was enhanced by moisture picked up off of Lake Erie as colder air poured in. Continued cold air advection behind the East Coast low into the 20th and early 21st allowed lake effect snow showers to continue, particularly in the higher elevations in southern Erie County and northern Crawford County. There were two periods in which snowfall was steadiest and at times heavy: During the afternoon and early evening hours on January 19th, especially across Erie County, and then from late-evening on the 19th through the evening on the 20th in locally intense lake effect bands, especially across southern Erie County and northern Crawford County. Snow rates reached 1 inch per hour at times with visibility occasionally falling below a quarter of a mile. Wind gusts up to 30 miles per hour led to patchy blowing snow, especially on the 20th. A trained spotter reported a prolonged storm total of 19 inches in Greenfield Township. Other select spotter reports include 16 inches in Edinboro, 15.6 inches in Venango, 13.5 inches in Meadville and 10.5 inches in North East.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak clipper moved across the Ohio Valley on January 19th bringing light snow to the region. As the clipper progressed east into the 20th it intensified off the East Coast as cold air poured into the region. The light snow with the clipper on January 19th was enhanced by moisture picked up off of Lake Erie as colder air poured in. Continued cold air advection behind the East Coast low into the 20th and early 21st allowed lake effect snow showers to continue. There were two periods in which snowfall was steadiest and at times heavy: During the afternoon and early evening hours on January 19th and then from the overnight and early morning hours through the evening on the 20th in locally intense lake effect bands. Snow rates reached 1 inch per hour at times with visibility occasionally falling below a quarter of a mile. Wind gusts up to 30 miles per hour led to patchy blowing snow, especially on the 20th. A trained spotter reported a prolonged storm total of 19 inches in Greenfield Township. Other select spotter reports include 16.4 inches in West Springfield, 16 inches in Edinboro, 15.4 inches in Lowville, 14.9 inches in Colt Station and 12.7 inches in North East Township. A CoCoRaHS observer in Corry recorded 12.2 inches of snow with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light precipitation, mainly in the form of rain, occurred across much of the region from the evening of the 23rd through the morning of the 24th. In the higher elevations of the southern Poconos, temperatures near freezing resulted in some light ice accretion across the Pocono Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 0.02 to 0.05 of liquid precipitation reported with freezing rain. Ice accretion confined mainly to the higher elevations across the Pocono Plateau. The Mount Pocono ASOS (KMPO) reported 0.06 of flat ice accretion, or about 0.02 radial.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on January 18th 2024, a surface low pressure system began to develop over the southeast CONUS. The surface low further developed as it tracked northeast along the Appalachian range. The deformation snowfall begin to develop just to the south along the northern WV areas and just south of the forecast area. The area of development did eventually move north as it overcame a low level dry area in the profile. However, the frontogenetic banding was weaker than forecasted and came in with lower snowfall amounts than forecasted through the day on Thursday. By Thursday evening and Thursday night, the surface low pushed off to the east and off the East Coast. Behind the front, during the early morning hours of January 19th, the lake enhanced upslope began as a post frontal trough assisted in snow shower development. The snow showers produced heavy snowfall at times and quickly made up for the lack of snow during the synoptic snowfall period.||During the upslope portion of the event, a strong narrow band of heavy snow showers formed right along the southwest border of Allegheny and Washington Counties in Pennsylvania and persisted there from 20Z on the 19th through 03Z on the 20th. This band, at it's max strength, was producing up to 2 inches per hour snowfall rates at times. It resulted in some snowfall amounts in PA counties such as Allegheny, Washington, Fayette, and Westmoreland well above expected totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports indicated that around 4 inches fell for a storm total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While surface low pressure tracked from the southeast CONUS off of the Mid-Atlantic coast on January 15th and 16th, an upper-level shortwave trough dug from the Upper Midwest into the Upper Ohio Valley. While moisture overall was not excessive, snow accumulation was highly efficient thanks to sufficiently cold temperatures and upward motion in the dendritic growth layer.  This led to an accumulation of several inches across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of two to three inches were received, with the high total of 4.1 inches received from Glassport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure center was located over the middle Mississippi Valley during the morning of January 9th, and tracked across Ohio and the eastern Great Lakes through the 10th. Many reports of rainfall in the 1 to 2 inch range were received from area gauges.  However, wind was the main impactful factor with this system.  Southeast downsloping wind resulted in numerous instances of tree and structural damage along west-facing slopes of Indiana, Westmoreland, and Fayette Counties.  Wind gusts of up to 66 MPH were measured at Latrobe.  Generally less severe wind gusts and damage were noted in other portions of southwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 51 MPH were noted at Indiana Airport, However, the call center reported that numerous power lines were blown down across the county during the day.  Specific examples were noted at Blairsville, Homer City, Indiana, and Marion Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough advanced across the Upper Ohio Valley on January 6th, as a surface low tracked across the Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic region. Snow overspread the region during the morning and continued into the early evening hours, with higher totals noted in the enhanced terrain. Some spotty freezing rain reports were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches. The high total was reported at Murraysville. A trace of icing was reported at Manor and Harrison City.

Southeast downsloping wind resulted in numerous instances of tree and structural damage along west-facing slopes of Indiana, Westmoreland, and Fayette Counties.  Wind gusts of up to 66 MPH were measured at Latrobe.  Generally less severe wind gusts and damage were noted in other portions of southwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusts up to 52 MPH were measured at Washington County Airport.

Wind gusts of up to 66 MPH were measured at Latrobe.  Generally less severe wind gusts and damage were noted in other portions of southwest Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 68 MPH were noted at Latrobe Airport. Multiple instances of downed trees and wires were noted in the county. Reports were received from the following locations: Bell Township, North Huntingdon Township, and  Latrobe. Roofs were blown off of two housed in Mount Pleasant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours on January 18th 2024, a surface low pressure system began to develop over the southeast CONUS. The surface low further developed as it tracked northeast along the Appalachian range. The deformation snowfall begin to develop just to the south along the northern WV areas and just south of the forecast area. The area of development did eventually move north as it overcame a low level dry area in the profile. However, the frontogenetic banding was weaker than forecasted and came in with lower snowfall amounts than forecasted through the day on Thursday. By Thursday evening and Thursday night, the surface low pushed off to the east and off the East Coast. Behind the front, during the early morning hours of January 19th, the lake enhanced upslope began as a post frontal trough assisted in snow shower development. The snow showers produced heavy snowfall at times and quickly made up for the lack of snow during the synoptic snowfall period.||During the upslope portion of the event, a strong narrow band of heavy snow showers formed right along the southwest border of Allegheny and Washington Counties in Pennsylvania and persisted there from 20Z on the 19th through 03Z on the 20th. This band, at it's max strength, was producing up to 2 inches per hour snowfall rates at times. It resulted in some snowfall amounts in PA counties such as Allegheny, Washington, Fayette, and Westmoreland well above expected totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports indicate 2 to 4.5 inches of accumulation, with Cooperstown on the higher end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While surface low pressure tracked from the southeast CONUS off of the Mid-Atlantic coast on January 15th and 16th, an upper-level shortwave trough dug from the Upper Midwest into the Upper Ohio Valley. While moisture overall was not excessive, snow accumulation was highly efficient thanks to sufficiently cold temperatures and upward motion in the dendritic growth layer.  This led to an accumulation of several inches across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to 2.5 inches of snow was reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough advanced across the Upper Ohio Valley on January 6th, as a surface low tracked across the Appalachians to the Mid-Atlantic region. Snow overspread the region during the morning and continued into the early evening hours, with higher totals noted in the enhanced terrain. Some spotty freezing rain reports were also noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of 1 to 3 inches were received. The highest total was 3.2 inches at North Versailles. Monroeville and Bethel Park also reported 3 inches. A trace of icing was also reported at West Mifflin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rather disorgranized system brought an extended period of generally light snow to central Pennsylvania from the afternoon of January 15 through the morning of January 16, 2024. Snowfall amounts ranged from an inch or less across the northwest mountains up to 4 to 5 inches across the far Lower Susquehanna Valley. Slightly higher amounts were observed across portions of Tioga County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 5 to 7 inches of snowfall across Tioga County from late evening hours of January 15 into January 16, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful surface low pressure system lifted from the middle Mississippi Valley and across Michigan during the January 12-13 period. Winds across the region strengthened on the 12th and continued into the 13th thanks to a tightening surface pressure gradient and the mixing down of strong wind aloft. Maximum gusts of up to 64 MPH were noted in southwest Pennsylvania, resulting in many instances of tree damage, as well as some structural damage. Hardest hit were the higher elevation counties of Westmoreland and Fayette, with one injury noted in the latter county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of up to 64 MPH were recorded at Connellsville Airport. Reports of downed trees were received from Gilmore and Uniontown.  In the latter location, multiple trees were involved, and siding and shingles were removed from a few houses.  A garage collapse also occurred in Connellsville. A tree fell on a home in Lemont Furnace. Also, at the Free Methodist Campground in Fairchance, a tree fell on a 70-year old man, breaking his leg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While surface low pressure tracked from the southeast CONUS off of the Mid-Atlantic coast on January 15th and 16th, an upper-level shortwave trough dug from the Upper Midwest into the Upper Ohio Valley. While moisture overall was not excessive, snow accumulation was highly efficient thanks to sufficiently cold temperatures and upward motion in the dendritic growth layer.  This led to an accumulation of several inches across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of two to three inches were received, with the high total of 4.1 inches received from Glassport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southeast winds resulted in widespread tidal flooding for much of the coast line and tidal waterways. The strong southeast to south winds resulted in a push of water up the Delaware Bay/River and through the back bays of the Atlantic coast. The outcome was the tidal inundation of roads and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries. There were road closures with the flood waters approaching and entering some vulnerable buildings. The tide gauge at Burlington reached a level of 10.59 feet MLLW.

The strong southeast to south winds resulted in a push of water up the Delaware Bay/River and through the back bays of the Atlantic coast. The outcome was the tidal inundation of roads and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries. There were many road closures with water entering vulnerable homes and businesses. The tide gauge at Bridesburg reached a level of 9.84 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at the Philadelphia Coast Guard Station reached a level of 9.41 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm advection ahead of low pressure pushing up the Ohio Valley produced a 4 to 6 hour period of moderate to heavy snow across the southern Allegheny Mountains of Pennsylvania during the morning of January 9, 2024. As much as 12 inches of snowfall was observed atop the Allegheny Front in eastern Somerset and eastern Cambria counties, with localized snowfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour. This system also produced 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain across the Cumberland and Susquehanna valleys, resulting in localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt led to flooding on Leafmore Road to the southeast of Saint Thomas, closing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large winter storm brought a period of moderate to heavy snowfall to the area beginning during the early afternoon hours on Saturday, January 6th 2024, spreading quickly from south to north over the region. There was a brief lull in the steady snow during the late evening hours on the 6th, followed by more steady to locally heavy snow during the morning hours on January 7th as a strengthening coastal low off the southern New England coast and inverted trough moved overhead. The snow tapered off and ended from west to east during the afternoon hours on Sunday, January 7th 2024. There was a very heavy snow band which extended west to east across Northeast Pennsylvania which brought a period of 2-3/hour snowfall rates for a time to some locations. Temperatures were mainly in the upper 20s to lower 30s through this event, but there was some brief mixing with sleet, freezing rain and drizzle over the Wyoming Valley during the late evening hours on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking to the west of Pennsylvania was preceded by warm advection and a strong low-level southeasterly jet of 70-80 knots. Gusts approaching 50 mph were observed across the Laurel Highlands January 12-13, 2024. Once this system passed and dragged a cold front across the area, blustery west-northwesterly winds behind the cold front gusted to 60 mph at a couple of spots in south-central Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 59 mph was recorded at KMDT.

The snow tapered off and ended from west to east during the afternoon hours on Sunday, January 7th 2024. There was a very heavy snow band which extended west to east across Northeast Pennsylvania which brought a period of 2-3/hour snowfall rates for a time to some locations. Temperatures were mainly in the upper 20s to lower 30s through this event, but there was some brief mixing with sleet, freezing rain and drizzle over the Wyoming Valley during the late evening hours on the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 5 and 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level trough of low pressure moved into the region while an area of low pressure moved from the Southeast off the mid-Atlantic coast and increased in strength. Between these two features, snow spread from south to north across the area beginning late in the evening of the 15th, continuing overnight and into the day of the 16th. The snow overnight combined with temperatures in the 20s led to a slippery morning commute for many on the 16th. A more potent shortwave moved into western NY enhancing lift and instability from late morning into the early afternoon on the 16th, which is when a steadier, moderate snow fell. Snow-to-liquid ratios were on the order of 15-20:1 for this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 5 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the region with steady southerly flow pushing water up into coastal communities both along the ocean and within the Delaware River/Bay and Chesapeake Bay. Water levels on the tidal Delaware River were higher due to 2 to 4+ inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread moderate to major flooding occurred along the tidal Delaware River and its tidal tributaries with both high tides on January 10th. There were road closures with the flood waters approaching and entering some vulnerable buildings.  The tide gauge at Newbold, Pennsylvania reached a level of 12.64 feet MLLW. The tide gauge at Burlington hit a record level of 11.99 feet MLLW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread light snowfall accumulations across the region, with a narrow mesoscale band of heavy snowfall occurring near and just south of I-78. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band only about 10 to 15 miles wide or less from Berks County eastward toward Raritan Bay in New Jersey with totals near a foot. A coating of snow also occurred well north and south of the band with amounts around 2 to 4. While total snowfall amounts within the band were rather impressive, generally falling within about 3-4 hours, impacts were limited as roadway temperatures hovered near freezing and the snowfall occurred late on a Friday night and early Saturday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 2 to 3 across much of the zone. The highest snowfall report was from Wynnewood with 4.2.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heat index reached 105��F at Philadelphia International Airport and Northeast Philadelphia Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in Bedford County on the evening of February 28, 2024 knocked down multiple trees and power lines in Monroe Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving area of low pressure pushed from the Lower Ohio Valley across the central Appalachians and off the Delmarva Coast from the evening of February 12 into the morning of February 13, 2024. This system pushed rain into Pennsylvania that quickly changed over to wet snow from north to south. The swath of heaviest snowfall extended from the middle Susquehanna Valley eastward into the Poconos, where 7 to 12 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moving into Montour County on the late evening hours of February 12 and into the morning hours of February 13, 2024 brought a brief period of initial rain before producing 6 to 8 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Tennessee Valley eastward across southern Virginia and off the Delmarva Coast, producing a swath of snow across central Pennsylvania from the evening of February 16 into the morning of February 17, 2024. A narrow swath of 6 to 10 inches of snow fell from the Laurel Highlands east-northeastward to the Lehigh Valley, with lesser amounts to the north and south of this heavier swath.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter weather event produced 2 to 4 inches of snow cross northern Centre County from the evening of February 16 into the early morning hours of February 17, 2024. As a result, a multi-vehicle accident involving 3 tractor-trailers occurred on I-80 eastbound in Boggs Township between the exits at PA-150 and PA-144. This resulted in the complete closure of the eastbound lanes for roughly 1.5 hours, with only one lane opening for another 2.5 hours before both eastbound lanes were opened. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers and squalls developed during the day in an anomalously cold airmass that advected in on a brisk northwesterly wind behind a departing winter storm. A few of the squalls dropped visibility significantly and posed a flash-freeze threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. A PennDOT RWIS station observed a sharp drop in visibility to less than 1/4 mile in a snow squall near Bellwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought widespread light to heavy snowfall accumulations across the region. The heaviest snowfall occurred across the southern Poconos with totals around a foot. A coating of snow also occurred all the way south into the Philadelphia urban corridor with amounts around 1 to 4. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged largely from around 11 to 13 across much of the county. The highest snowfall report was from Albrightsville with 14.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds swept across the region in the late afternoon of February 15, 2024. This was caused by a combination of factors: deep mixing near the ground, daytime heating from the sun, and a powerful low-level jet stream with winds between 50 and 60 knots. Some of the strongest wind gusts were associated with passing showers that pulled the strong winds down to the ground. The highest gust measured was 58 mph, recorded at Latrobe Airport during a shower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranging from 46mph to 54mph were measured across Allegheny County. The highest wind gust was measured at 1940Z at KAGC. There was minor roof damage to a warehouse in Carnegie, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of February 16th, a narrow band of heavy snow caused trouble for some communities. Most areas got between 2 and 4 inches of snow. But a concentrated band of snow showers, only 5 to 10 miles wide, stretched from Coshocton, Ohio all the way to Allegheny County, Pennsylvania. This band dumped significantly more snow, with accumulations ranging from 6 to 11 inches. The localized band evolved due to a slow moving area of frontogenesis between 925mb to 700mb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 4-6 inches of snow fell across Allegheny County. The heaviest amount occurred along a narrow band.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds swept across the region in the late afternoon of February 15, 2024. This was caused by a combination of factors: deep mixing near the ground, daytime heating from the sun, and a powerful low-level jet stream with winds between 50 and 60 knots. Some of the strongest wind gusts were associated with passing showers that pulled the strong winds down to the ground. The highest gust measured was 58 mph, recorded at Latrobe Airport during a shower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Butler County Airport measured 46mph at 1915Z. A few trees were reported down in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This day, May 17th, will be remembered as one of the more unusual tornado days in southwest Pennsylvania. Although overall levels of instability were fairly low, enough of that parameter, plus shear, was concentrated in the 0 to 3 km layer to allow for the formation of low-topped supercells during the early to mid-afternoon hours. Interaction with a surface boundary allowed for tornadogenesis in a few of the storms as low-level streamwise vorticity was ingested into updrafts. Three of the tornadoes were fairly weak, at EF0 level, with one EF1 impacting areas within the Pittsburgh city limits, including the Pittsburgh Zoo. No straight-line wind or hail reports were received, as the storms were small and as the environment did not support those impacts. One isolated flood report was also received in Allegheny County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornadic damage associated with this mesocyclone occurred sporadically from near Elrama to eastern parts of Elizabeth, with several gaps in the damage swath.||After the tornado crossed into Allegheny County, sporadic tree damage was noted, especially in the area of Boston Hollow Rd and Mansfield Rd. In this segment of the circulation track, the swath of damage was narrow, and characterized by uprooted trees and snapped limbs.||Video of this sporadically contracted/intensified circulation was offered, confirming an occasional tornadic ground circulation to supplement the reports of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level low tracked through the Great Lakes early February 28th and into A strong upper-level low tracked through the Great Lakes and into the Ohio River Valley early on February 28th. Nocturnal convection was mainly elevated before 5 AM, with a few hail reports noted. However, with a surge of warm air after 6 AM, there was just enough instability to create a few instances of wind damage. Based on sounding profiles from PIT and ILN (06Z and 12Z profiles), shear was strong within the low-levels, ranging from 50kts to 60kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo of a large pine down on Lincoln Street was provided.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving area of low pressure pushed from the Lower Ohio Valley across the central Appalachians and off the Delmarva Coast from the evening of February 12 into the morning of February 13, 2024. This system pushed rain into Pennsylvania that quickly changed over to wet snow from north to south. The swath of heaviest snowfall extended from the middle Susquehanna Valley eastward into the Poconos, where 7 to 12 inches of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought an initial period rain to Adams County during the late evening hours of February 12th before producing 5 to 7 inches of snowfall throughout the early morning hours of February 13, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms moved through the area, where there was limited instability yet significant wind shear supportive of rotation in storm cells. A storm early in the evening did produce a brief tornado in West Damascus in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near the intersection of Oregon Turnpike and Cochecton Turnpike in West Damascus in the early evening of May 27th. The tornado initially produced minor tree and crop damage and lifted some debris into powerlines. It then|continued eastward and crossed Cochecton Turnpike as well as Rutledgedale Road, uprooted a very large tree, and lofted additional debris into the tree. The tornado then crossed through a farm and caused significant roof and structural damage to a couple of barns. This is where the tornado was at its peak strength, with a metal roof partially lifted off a barn and a large wooden door ripped off another barn. Debris was also lofted down a hill roughly 100 feet away. Some minor tree and crop damage was observed down this hill, which was the last visible damage of the survey and the determined location where the tornado lifted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between about daybreak and nightfall on August 6th, a wavy surface cold front drifted generally southward from northern OH and near the NY/PA line to southern OH and the northern Mid-Atlantic states. This front separated a warm and humid air mass to its north from a warmer and more-humid air mass to its south. Multiple lines of multicell thunderstorms persisted southeastward across northwest PA during the late morning through early afternoon of the 6th and produced hail up to penny-size and isolated straight-line convective wind damage in Crawford County. A separate line of multicell thunderstorms persisted eastward across northwest PA during the early evening of the 6th and produced additional isolated straight-line convective wind damage in Crawford County. All of these storms encountered weak to moderate MUCAPE and moderate to strong effective bulk shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed about eight-miles SSW of Linesville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged, crippling, and in some places historic lake effect event took place across the Northwest Pennsylvania snowbelt to end November and start December in 2024. The event ramped up overnight November 28th and 29th as a band of very heavy snow with snow rates up to 3 inches per hour took shape on a westerly wind. This band initially took aim at much of Erie County and northern Crawford County. During the daytime on November 29th the winds backed to a more west-southwest direction which focused the heaviest snow closer to the Erie County lakeshore. Winds turned more westerly during the evening, directing intense bands with snow rates up to 2 inches per hour back into much of Erie County, along with far northern Crawford County. Lake effect snow lifted northeast and out of the area into the day on November 30th as winds shifted southwest. This provided a 12 to 24 hour break in the snow before activity returned December 1st. The first portion of this event led to significant impacts across Northwest Pennsylvania, particularly in Erie County. Large stretches of I-90 and I-86 were closed in Erie County from the afternoon the 29th through much of the 30th. On other roads, many accidents were reported and some cars became stranded in the snow. Erie County declared a state of emergency due to the snow event. The heavy weight of the snow caused some tree and vegetation damage, with some structural damage such as roof collapses also noted. Peak totals from November 29th and 30th included 43.5 inches in North East, 42.2 inches in Girard, 42 inches in Greenfield Township, 35.1 inches at Erie International Airport and 17.6 inches in Spring Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged, crippling, and in some places historic lake effect event took place across the Northwest Pennsylvania snowbelt to end November and start December in 2024. The event ramped up overnight November 28th and 29th as a band of very heavy snow with snow rates up to 3 inches per hour took shape on a westerly wind. This band of heavy snow focused on the northern half of Erie County during the day November 29th before spreading slightly farther inland that evening, with rates continuing to exceed 2 inches per hour at times. Lake effect snow lifted northeast and out of the area into the day on November 30th as winds shifted southwest. This provided a 12 to 24 hour break in the snow before activity returned December 1st. The first portion of this event led to significant impacts across northern Erie County as the amount of snow that fell in such a short duration was impossible for road crews to keep up with. Large stretches of I-90 and I-86 were closed in Erie County from the afternoon on the 29th through much of the 30th. On other roads, many accidents were reported and numerous cars became stranded in the snow. Erie County requested assistance from the Pennsylvania National Guard to help reach stranded motorists and to clear roads. Local snowmobile clubs were also utilized to help with the large number of stranded motorists. Erie County declared a state of emergency due to the snow event. The heavy weight of the snow caused some tree and vegetation damage, with some structural damage such as roof collapses also noted. Peak spotter totals from November 29th and 30th included 2 inches in Greene Township, 40.5 inches in McKean, 40 inches in Milcreek Township, 38 inches in Greenfield Township, 19 inches in Edinboro, and decreasing to 7.5 inches in Corry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between about daybreak and nightfall on August 6th, a wavy surface cold front drifted generally southward from northern OH and near the NY/PA line to southern OH and the northern Mid-Atlantic states. This front separated a warm and humid air mass to its north from a warmer and more-humid air mass to its south. Multiple lines of multicell thunderstorms persisted southeastward across northwest PA during the late morning through early afternoon of the 6th and produced hail up to penny-size and isolated straight-line convective wind damage in Crawford County. A separate line of multicell thunderstorms persisted eastward across northwest PA during the early evening of the 6th and produced additional isolated straight-line convective wind damage in Crawford County. All of these storms encountered weak to moderate MUCAPE and moderate to strong effective bulk shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees uprooted in Geneva.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break in the lake effect during much of the day and into the night of November 30th, lake effect returned by early in the morning December 1st as winds shifted out of the west-northwest, directing lake effect bands back onshore across Northwest Pennsylvania. Lake effect snow, heavy at times with rates in excess of 1 inch per hour within bands, continued through December 3rd. On top of the already significant impacts felt from the lake effect that occurred at the end of November, this additional snowfall prolonged snow removal and general recovery efforts, while also leading to additional damage caused by the weight of snow structures, with additional roof collapses reported as snow continued to add up the first days of December. A trained spotter in Edinboro reported an additional 25.5 inches of snow with this second portion of the prolonged lake effect event. Other select spotter reports include: 24.8 inches in Greenfield Township, 22.8 inches in Venango Township, and 16.6 inches in North East Township. This pushed 5-day snowfall totals, including the first lake effect event that began on November 28th and some lingering activity through December 3rd, to over 5 feet in parts of Erie County. Selected 5-day totals include: 63.8 inches in Girard, 61 inches in Greenfield Township, 59.7 inches in North East Township, 50 inches in Erie, 45.5 inches in Edinboro, and 38 inches in Venango Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief break in the lake effect during much of the day and into the night of November 30th, lake effect returned by early in the morning December 1st as winds shifted out of the west-northwest, directing lake effect bands back onshore across Erie County. Lake effect snow, heavy at times with rates in excess of 1 inch per hour within bands, continued into early December 2nd. Lighter and less organized lake effect continued at times through the rest of December 2nd and 3rd, though this snow did not reach heavy snow criteria. On top of the already significant impacts felt from the lake effect that occurred at the end of November, this additional snowfall prolonged snow removal and general recovery efforts, while also leading to additional damage caused by the weight of the snow on structures, with additional roof collapses reported as snow continued to add up the first days of December. A trained spotter in North East reported 12 inches of snow December 1st into the 2nd. Other select spotter reports include 10 inches in Northwest Harborcreek and 9 inches in Erie. Erie International Airport reported 6 inches of snow. This pushed 5-day snowfall totals, including the first lake effect event that began on November 28th and some lingering activity through December 3rd, to over 5 feet in parts of Erie County. Selected 5-day totals include: 63.8 inches in Girard, 59.7 inches in North East Township, 55.2 inches in Northwest Harborcreek and 50 inches in Erie. Erie International Airport measured 49.4 inches of snow between November 28th and December 3rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey confirmed that an EF1 tornado impacted Mahanoy City the evening of May 27, 2024. The estimated maximum wind speed was approximately 90mph, with a maximum path width of approximately 75 yards. The path length was just over 1.5 miles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey confirmed that an EF1 tornado impacted Mahanoy City the evening of May 27, 2024. The tornado touched down just west of Mahanoy City around 7:38pm. Several trees were uprooted as the tornado traveled northeastward into Mahanoy City and crossed East Railroad Street. Shingles and wooden debris were lofted into the air, and several impalements of wood into roofs and into the ground were observed. Several windows on the local elementary school were damaged as well. The tornado crossed train tracks just north of Mahanoy City and uprooted several additional trees before lifting after it crossed Glendon Road around 7:44 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break in the lake effect during much of the day and into the night of November 30th, lake effect returned by early in the morning December 1st as winds shifted out of the west-northwest, directing lake effect bands back onshore across Northwest Pennsylvania. Lake effect snow, heavy at times with rates in excess of 1 inch per hour within bands, continued through December 3rd. On top of the already significant impacts felt from the lake effect that occurred at the end of November, this additional snowfall prolonged snow removal and general recovery efforts, while also leading to additional damage caused by the weight of snow structures, with additional roof collapses reported as snow continued to add up the first days of December. A trained spotter in Edinboro reported an additional 25.5 inches of snow with this second portion of the prolonged lake effect event. Other select spotter reports include: 24.8 inches in Greenfield Township, 22.8 inches in Venango Township, and 16.6 inches in North East Township. This pushed 5-day snowfall totals, including the first lake effect event that began on November 28th and some lingering activity through December 3rd, to over 5 feet in parts of Erie County. Selected 5-day totals include: 63.8 inches in Girard, 61 inches in Greenfield Township, 59.7 inches in North East Township, 50 inches in Erie, 45.5 inches in Edinboro, and 38 inches in Venango Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief break in the lake effect during much of the day and into the night of November 30th, lake effect returned by early in the morning December 1st as winds shifted out of the west-northwest, directing lake effect bands back onshore across Erie County. Lake effect snow, heavy at times with rates in excess of 1 inch per hour within bands, continued through the early evening on December 3rd. The snow was generally most organized and intense from the afternoon of December 1st through early December 2nd, though was heavy at times within the most bands through the 3rd. On top of the already significant impacts felt from the lake effect that occurred at the end of November, this additional snowfall prolonged snow removal and general recovery efforts, while also leading to additional damage caused by the weight of the snow on structures, with additional roof collapses reported as snow continued to add up the first days of December across northern Erie County. A trained spotter in Edinboro reported 25.5 inches of snow with this portion of the lake effect event. Other select spotter reports include 24.8 inches in Greenfield Township, 19 inches in Greene Township, and 13.9 inches in Union City. A CoCoRaHS observer in Corry reported 13.7 inches. This pushed 5-day snowfall totals from November 28th through December 3rd, to: 61 inches in Greene Township, 54.7 inches in Greenfield Township, 45.5 inches in Edinboro, and 24.2 inches in Corry, the last measured by a CoCoRaHS observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, a low drifted eastward across southern Lake Huron during the afternoon through early evening of the 17th. The attendant warm front moved northeastward across the Lake Ontario region and toward the Upper St. Lawrence River Valley. Simultaneously, the attendant cold front moved eastward from southeastern Lower MI and northwest OH to central Lake Erie and north-central OH as northwest PA remained within the warm and humid sector. Scattered multicell thunderstorms impacted northwest PA during the early afternoon through early evening hours. The storms generated isolated straight-line convective wind damage amidst moderate MUCAPE and effective bulk shear, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed about one-mile northwest of Platea.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, an occluded low moved north-northeastward from near western Lake Superior to northern ON, west of James Bay, during the late morning of the 22nd through the wee hours of the morning of the 23rd. Simultaneously, the trailing cold front advanced generally eastward from southeastern Lower MI to the western NY to northeast OH corridor, just east and southeast of Lake Erie. Clusters and lines of multicell thunderstorms developed ahead of the cold front, and produced straight-line convective wind damage in portions of northwest PA during the late morning and early afternoon of the 22nd. The storms occurred in a warm/moist sector comprised of primarily weak to moderate MUCAPE, moderate to strong DCAPE, and moderate to strong effective bulk shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed in Hartstown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed along Shady Avenue in Conneaut Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave and surface trough approached eastern Pennsylvania. The incoming disturbance encountered a very warm, moist, and unstable airmass. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching MCV, coupled with ample instability and moisture during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 16, 2024 allowed for convection to begin forming across the Lower Susquehanna Valley.  These thunderstorms quickly began producing instances of severe wind damage across the Lower Susquehanna Valley with isolated instances of small-to-marginally severe hail as they continued tracking northeastward into the Lehigh Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked a tree down onto Hedgewick Lane in East Prospect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system (MCS) moved into the Northeast and brought widespread showers and thunderstorms with it. With this system moving into the region in the afternoon and evening, daytime heating helped create an unstable environment. Enough shear was present to support long-lasting storms. In Northeastern Pennsylvania, there were no reports of tornadoes, but there were numerous reports of wind damage ranging from trees being knocked down to roofs being ripped off of buildings. A man, in his home, survived a lightning strike and only suffered minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of showers and thunderstorms rolled across Central Pennsylvania during the early to mid morning hours of May 23, 2024, producing sporadic wind damage. Then, that evening, an isolated thunderstorm formed over southern York County as a cold front slipped southward across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a large tree across McAlveys Fort Road in Jackson Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system was the driving force of scattered storms across Northeast Pennsylvania. Warm and moist air contributed to several rounds of heavy rain producing thunderstorms over the northern tier counties. Rainfall amounts in a short period of time totalled between 2 and 3 inches leading to localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water is flowing over Route 467 in several locations along Johnson Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front helped to focus the remnants of tropical cyclone Beryl which brought steady rainfall and embedded thunderstorms to the region. A cold front then brought a second round of thunderstorms in the evening. Thunderstorms with intense rainfall produced narrow bands of 2-4 inches of rain around the area leading to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported water over several roadways in New Albany.

In Northeastern Pennsylvania, there were no reports of tornadoes, but there were numerous reports of wind damage ranging from trees being knocked down to roofs being ripped off of buildings. A man, in his home, survived a lightning strike and only suffered minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave and surface trough approached eastern Pennsylvania. The incoming disturbance encountered a very warm, moist, and unstable airmass. The result was several strong to severe thunderstorms developing, producing damaging wind gusts and downing several trees and power lines in eastern Pennsylvania. Large hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Bethel Twp: Club Rd and Faust Rd - Wires Down. Time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought started in portions of western Pennsylvania on July 16th. This would be continue to some degree through the remainder of the month for portions of the counties in the forecast area, with some improvement in Greene and Washington counties as a result of 2-4 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An afternoon trough passage supported a few clusters of thunderstorms in an environment characterized by 1000-2000 J/kg of surface-based instability and up to 1500 J/kg of downdraft CAPE. This supported the development of downdraft-aided wind gusts that caused several instances of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on to McQuistion Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An environment characterized by strong synoptic support for lift, up to 2000 J/kg of surface-based CAPE, and good synoptic support for lift fostered the development of a few broken lines of thunderstorms during the afternoon of July 24th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near Glen Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in western Ohio on April 17th along a cold front before tracking east into western Pennsylvania. Behind the cold front, a 50kt low-level jet was measured on the KPBZ radar at 3,000ft and surface based instability ranged between 500J/kg to 1,000J/kg. As a result, widespread wind damage was noted with this passing system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees reported down in Cranberry Township.

Behind the cold front, a 50kt low-level jet was measured on the KPBZ radar at 3,000ft and surface based instability ranged between 500J/kg to 1,000J/kg. As a result, widespread wind damage was noted with this passing system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report and video of large tree down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection developed along passing cold front and trough during the late afternoon of April 14th over Lake Erie. The line matured and slowly progressed south and created severe hail and flooding due to training. Effective shear was maximized between 18Z and 00Z over northern PA with a low-level jet originated from west to east.  Surface instability likely ranged from 500J/kg to 1000J/kg and shear from the surface to 500mb likely ranged between 45kt to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough and an associated frontal zone crossed through the|Mid-South and took aim on the OH Valley. Strong forcing associated the energy aloft coupled with a gradually destabilizing boundary layer out ahead it set the stage for areas of locally heavy showers and thunderstorms for several hours and prompted dangerous flash flooding. A flash flood emergency was issued for portions of Allegheny County when 4 to 5 inches of rain fell on the community of Oakdale, PA in less than 6 hours.  Elsewhere, flash flooding occurred along small tributaries with widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 1006 was flooded between Route 65 and Route 288.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms prompted a widespread flash flood problems across the Ohio River Valley on April 2nd to April 3rd. The synoptic pattern across the CONUS was a slow moving and deep elongated trough over the Great Plains phasing with a ejecting trough over the Midwest that created an area of converging moisture over eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania. Overall, over a span of 4 days, four to five an half inches of rain fell across the region. Not only was small tributaries impact, but the main river reached moderate flood stage. ||Along with flooding, there was a brief period of severe storms that tracked along eastern Ohio on the evening of April 2nd. A number of tornadoes touched down and were surveyed. Special soundings released by PBZ and ILN depict effective instability over eastern Ohio (1200J/kg to 1700J/kg), effective low-level shear (+50kts), and low-level helicity (300m2/s2) for rotating updrafts just before storms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several streams out of their banks in the Moon Township and Robinson area.

A flash flood emergency was issued for portions of Allegheny County when 4 to 5 inches of rain fell on the community of Oakdale, PA in less than 6 hours.  Elsewhere, flash flooding occurred along small tributaries with widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northbound Route 18 closed between Water Dam Road and Garner Run Road due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms prompted a widespread flash flood problems across the Ohio River Valley on April 2nd to April 3rd. The synoptic pattern across the CONUS was a slow moving and deep elongated trough over the Great Plains phasing with a ejecting trough over the Midwest that created an area of converging moisture over eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania. Overall, over a span of 4 days, four to five an half inches of rain fell across the region. Not only was small tributaries impact, but the main river reached moderate flood stage. ||Along with flooding, there was a brief period of severe storms that tracked along eastern Ohio on the evening of April 2nd. A number of tornadoes touched down and were surveyed. Special soundings released by PBZ and ILN depict effective instability over eastern Ohio (1200J/kg to 1700J/kg), effective low-level shear (+50kts), and low-level helicity (300m2/s2) for rotating updrafts just before storms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 28 south near Sharpsburg starting to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in western Ohio on April 17th along a cold front before tracking east into western Pennsylvania. Behind the cold front, a 50kt low-level jet was measured on the KPBZ radar at 3,000ft and surface based instability ranged between 500J/kg to 1,000J/kg. As a result, widespread wind damage was noted with this passing system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in New Kensington with power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection developed along passing cold front and trough during the late afternoon of April 14th over Lake Erie. The line matured and slowly progressed south and created severe hail and flooding due to training. Effective shear was maximized between 18Z and 00Z over northern PA with a low-level jet originated from west to east.  Surface instability likely ranged from 500J/kg to 1000J/kg and shear from the surface to 500mb likely ranged between 45kt to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported by a trained spotter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Green Valley Road is underwater near Route 40 in Taylorstown, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms prompted a widespread flash flood problems across the Ohio River Valley on April 2nd to April 3rd. The synoptic pattern across the CONUS was a slow moving and deep elongated trough over the Great Plains phasing with a ejecting trough over the Midwest that created an area of converging moisture over eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania. Overall, over a span of 4 days, four to five an half inches of rain fell across the region. Not only was small tributaries impact, but the main river reached moderate flood stage. ||Along with flooding, there was a brief period of severe storms that tracked along eastern Ohio on the evening of April 2nd. A number of tornadoes touched down and were surveyed. Special soundings released by PBZ and ILN depict effective instability over eastern Ohio (1200J/kg to 1700J/kg), effective low-level shear (+50kts), and low-level helicity (300m2/s2) for rotating updrafts just before storms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creek out of its banks and covering the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection developed along passing cold front and trough during the late afternoon of April 14th over Lake Erie. The line matured and slowly progressed south and created severe hail and flooding due to training. Effective shear was maximized between 18Z and 00Z over northern PA with a low-level jet originated from west to east.  Surface instability likely ranged from 500J/kg to 1000J/kg and shear from the surface to 500mb likely ranged between 45kt to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough and an associated frontal zone crossed through the|Mid-South and took aim on the OH Valley. Strong forcing associated the energy aloft coupled with a gradually destabilizing boundary layer out ahead it set the stage for areas of locally heavy showers and thunderstorms for several hours and prompted dangerous flash flooding. A flash flood emergency was issued for portions of Allegheny County when 4 to 5 inches of rain fell on the community of Oakdale, PA in less than 6 hours.  Elsewhere, flash flooding occurred along small tributaries with widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 18 at Gretna Road flooded.
