EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV passing over Lake Erie and New York state helped to kick off scattered convection over northern and western Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of June 17, 2024. In a very warm and humid airmass with moderately high CAPE but low shear, a few of these storms produced wind damage as they pushed into the Allegheny Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated at 60 mph knocked down a tree along Morann Avenue near Eureka Street south of Houtzdale.

A flash flood emergency was issued for portions of Allegheny County when 4 to 5 inches of rain fell on the community of Oakdale, PA in less than 6 hours.  Elsewhere, flash flooding occurred along small tributaries with widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 4005 (Old Pulaski Road) between Glen Road and Nashua Road in Pulaski Township - Closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection developed along passing cold front and trough during the late afternoon of April 14th over Lake Erie. The line matured and slowly progressed south and created severe hail and flooding due to training. Effective shear was maximized between 18Z and 00Z over northern PA with a low-level jet originated from west to east.  Surface instability likely ranged from 500J/kg to 1000J/kg and shear from the surface to 500mb likely ranged between 45kt to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported along Old Ash Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms prompted a widespread flash flood problems across the Ohio River Valley on April 2nd to April 3rd. The synoptic pattern across the CONUS was a slow moving and deep elongated trough over the Great Plains phasing with a ejecting trough over the Midwest that created an area of converging moisture over eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania. Overall, over a span of 4 days, four to five an half inches of rain fell across the region. Not only was small tributaries impact, but the main river reached moderate flood stage. ||Along with flooding, there was a brief period of severe storms that tracked along eastern Ohio on the evening of April 2nd. A number of tornadoes touched down and were surveyed. Special soundings released by PBZ and ILN depict effective instability over eastern Ohio (1200J/kg to 1700J/kg), effective low-level shear (+50kts), and low-level helicity (300m2/s2) for rotating updrafts just before storms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Caney Valley Road partially flooded with one bridge under water near Henry Clay Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCV passing over Lake Erie and New York state helped to kick off scattered convection over northern and western Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of June 17, 2024. In a very warm and humid airmass with moderately high CAPE but low shear, a few of these storms produced wind damage as they pushed into the Allegheny Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated at 60 mph knocked down a tree onto South Kendall Avenue, blocking both lanes of traffic southeast of Bradford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged, crippling, and in some places historic lake effect event took place across the Northwest Pennsylvania snowbelt to end November and start December in 2024. The event ramped up overnight November 28th and 29th as a band of very heavy snow with snow rates up to 3 inches per hour took shape on a westerly wind. This band initially took aim at much of Erie County and northern Crawford County. During the daytime on November 29th the winds backed to a more west-southwest direction which focused the heaviest snow closer to the Erie County lakeshore. Winds turned more westerly during the evening, directing intense bands with snow rates up to 2 inches per hour back into much of Erie County, along with far northern Crawford County. Lake effect snow lifted northeast and out of the area into the day on November 30th as winds shifted southwest. This provided a 12 to 24 hour break in the snow before activity returned December 1st. The first portion of this event led to significant impacts across Northwest Pennsylvania, particularly in Erie County. Large stretches of I-90 and I-86 were closed in Erie County from the afternoon the 29th through much of the 30th. On other roads, many accidents were reported and some cars became stranded in the snow. Erie County declared a state of emergency due to the snow event. The heavy weight of the snow caused some tree and vegetation damage, with some structural damage such as roof collapses also noted. Peak totals from November 29th and 30th included 43.5 inches in North East, 42.2 inches in Girard, 42 inches in Greenfield Township, 35.1 inches at Erie International Airport and 17.6 inches in Spring Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged, crippling, and in some places historic lake effect event took place across the Northwest Pennsylvania snowbelt to end November and start December in 2024. The event ramped up overnight November 28th and 29th as a band of very heavy snow with snow rates up to 3 inches per hour took shape on a westerly wind. This band of heavy snow focused on the northern half of Erie County during the day November 29th before spreading slightly farther inland that evening, with rates continuing to exceed 2 inches per hour at times. Lake effect snow lifted northeast and out of the area into the day on November 30th as winds shifted southwest. This provided a 12 to 24 hour break in the snow before activity returned December 1st. The first portion of this event led to significant impacts across northern Erie County as the amount of snow that fell in such a short duration was impossible for road crews to keep up with. Large stretches of I-90 and I-86 were closed in Erie County from the afternoon on the 29th through much of the 30th. On other roads, many accidents were reported and numerous cars became stranded in the snow. Erie County requested assistance from the Pennsylvania National Guard to help reach stranded motorists and to clear roads. Local snowmobile clubs were also utilized to help with the large number of stranded motorists. Erie County declared a state of emergency due to the snow event. The heavy weight of the snow caused some tree and vegetation damage, with some structural damage such as roof collapses also noted. Peak spotter totals from November 29th and 30th included 43.5 inches in North East, 42.2 inches in Girard, 40.2 inches in Northwest Harborcreek, and 35.1 inches at Erie International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance brought a couple of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of Northeastern Pennsylvania. There were multiple reports of trees down in the Wyoming Valley and Poconos as damaging winds were the main hazard with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires in Porter Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training showers created isolated flooding in portions of the region early Monday morning (April 1st). Convection originated in the Midwest along a stationary boundary. With zonal flow over Illinois, Indiana, and Ohio, due to an elongated trough over the West Coast, convection trained from west to east. CoCoRaHS observers measured 1.50 to 2 inches of rain along I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Greene School District football field along Hargus Creek, McCourtney Run flooded. Golden Oaks Road is closed as a result of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in western Ohio on April 17th along a cold front before tracking east into western Pennsylvania. Behind the cold front, a 50kt low-level jet was measured on the KPBZ radar at 3,000ft and surface based instability ranged between 500J/kg to 1,000J/kg. As a result, widespread wind damage was noted with this passing system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 inch hail.

Not only was small tributaries impact, but the main river reached moderate flood stage. ||Along with flooding, there was a brief period of severe storms that tracked along eastern Ohio on the evening of April 2nd. A number of tornadoes touched down and were surveyed. Special soundings released by PBZ and ILN depict effective instability over eastern Ohio (1200J/kg to 1700J/kg), effective low-level shear (+50kts), and low-level helicity (300m2/s2) for rotating updrafts just before storms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 4012 closed near West Sunbury Road due to flooding. State Route 308 closed between Cemetery Road and Deer Road due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in western Ohio on April 17th along a cold front before tracking east into western Pennsylvania. Behind the cold front, a 50kt low-level jet was measured on the KPBZ radar at 3,000ft and surface based instability ranged between 500J/kg to 1,000J/kg. As a result, widespread wind damage was noted with this passing system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of 1 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of convection developed along passing cold front and trough during the late afternoon of April 14th over Lake Erie. The line matured and slowly progressed south and created severe hail and flooding due to training. Effective shear was maximized between 18Z and 00Z over northern PA with a low-level jet originated from west to east.  Surface instability likely ranged from 500J/kg to 1000J/kg and shear from the surface to 500mb likely ranged between 45kt to 55kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough and an associated frontal zone crossed through the|Mid-South and took aim on the OH Valley. Strong forcing associated the energy aloft coupled with a gradually destabilizing boundary layer out ahead it set the stage for areas of locally heavy showers and thunderstorms for several hours and prompted dangerous flash flooding. A flash flood emergency was issued for portions of Allegheny County when 4 to 5 inches of rain fell on the community of Oakdale, PA in less than 6 hours.  Elsewhere, flash flooding occurred along small tributaries with widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicles stuck in water along Freeport Road and Delafield Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a brief break in the lake effect during much of the day and into the night of November 30th, lake effect returned by early in the morning December 1st as winds shifted out of the west-northwest, directing lake effect bands back onshore across Northwest Pennsylvania. Lake effect snow, heavy at times with rates in excess of 1 inch per hour within bands, continued through December 3rd. On top of the already significant impacts felt from the lake effect that occurred at the end of November, this additional snowfall prolonged snow removal and general recovery efforts, while also leading to additional damage caused by the weight of snow structures, with additional roof collapses reported as snow continued to add up the first days of December. A trained spotter in Edinboro reported an additional 25.5 inches of snow with this second portion of the prolonged lake effect event. Other select spotter reports include: 24.8 inches in Greenfield Township, 22.8 inches in Venango Township, and 16.6 inches in North East Township. This pushed 5-day snowfall totals, including the first lake effect event that began on November 28th and some lingering activity through December 3rd, to over 5 feet in parts of Erie County. Selected 5-day totals include: 63.8 inches in Girard, 61 inches in Greenfield Township, 59.7 inches in North East Township, 50 inches in Erie, 45.5 inches in Edinboro, and 38 inches in Venango Township.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a break in the lake effect November 30th into December 1st, lake effect returned by the evening hours on December 1st as winds shifted out of the west-northwest, directing lake effect bands back into northern portions of Crawford County. Lake effect snow, heavy at times with rates in excess of 1 inch per hour within bands, continued through the early evening on December 3rd. This led to periods of reduced visibility and snow-covered roads, which impacted travel conditions, especially across northern portions of the county. A trained spotter in Venango Township reported 22.8 inches of snow from December 1st through 3rd, with 9.2 inches in Athens Township. This pushed 5-day snowfall totals from November 28th through December 3rd to 38 inches in Venango Township and 14.1 inches in Athens Township. Amounts quickly dropped off farther south across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through Northwest Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of December 5th, accompanied by a band of moderate to heavy snow showers and gusty winds up to 40 MPH. Lake effect snow quickly developed into the morning of the 5th as winds shifted more northwesterly and temperatures fell through the 20s behind this front. An initial burst of lake effect snow occurred during the early to mid-morning hours on the 5th, with rates up to 1 inch per hour and 35 to 45 MPH wind gusts common, with up to 55 MPH near Lake Erie. This led to significant impacts to the morning commute, with many local school districts and businesses closing for the day and whiteout conditions and drifting snow reported. After a lull, lake effect snow turned moderate to heavy again during the afternoon and evening hours, leading to impacts to the evening commute. The lake effect snow gradually became less intense and more disorganized by early in the morning on December 6th as winds also subsided significantly. The peak snowfall from this event was 29 inches, measured by a trained spotter in Union City. A CoCoRaHS observer in Corry measured 26.3 inches. Selected spotter reports include 20.7 inches in Venango Township, 17.9 inches in Edinboro, 17.8 inches in Cochranton, and 16.4 inches in Meadville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through Northwest Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of December 5th, accompanied by a band of moderate to heavy snow showers and gusty winds up to 40 MPH. Lake effect snow quickly developed into the morning of the 5th as winds shifted more northwesterly and temperatures fell into the 20s behind this front. The main window of impactful snow and blowing snow occurred through the mid-morning hours on the 5th with significant impacts to the morning commute. During the commute, 40 to 50 MPH wind gusts were common with a peak gust of 55 MPH measured at Erie International Airport. This, combined with moderate to heavy falling snow and tumbling temperatures lead to a period of near-blizzard conditions with very low visibility and extensive drifting snow. Travel was very difficult. Many local schools and businesses closed for the day, and numerous reports, pictures, and videos from local media, the public, and storm chasers showed whiteout conditions and snow-covered roads, with accidents and snowbound vehicles reported. After a lull during the late morning and early afternoon hours, some additional lake effect snow showers returned through the afternoon and into the evening of December 5th. Snow became lighter and more intermittent through the night, with some lingering snow showers on December 6th that did not reach heavy snow criteria. The heaviest snow fell inland from Lake Erie, with the strong winds further hampering measurement efforts. Snow amounts were marginal, with spotter reports ranging from 4.3 inches at Erie International Airport to 4 inches in Erie and Harborcreek, 3.8 inches in Northwest Harborcreek and 3 inches in Girard. While amounts of new snow were only a few inches, the extensive pre-existing snow pack and strong winds, along with timing during the morning commute, led to a period of high-impact winter weather. Some additional roof collapses were noted as new snow and strong winds combined with a heavy pre-existing snowpack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As strong low pressure tracked out of the Ohio Valley, across western Ohio and into the Great Lakes, a strong cold front moved east across the area the evening of December 29th. Strong winds occurred behind this cold front, with some sites measuring wind gusts of 58 MPH or stronger and some downed trees and power-lines reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64 MPH wind gust was measured at 931 PM at Meadville Airport. Local emergency management reported numerous downed trees and powerlines across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front began moving across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of June 30, 2024 with severe thunderstorms forming ahead of and along the frontal boundary. This frontal boundary, with ample moisture and instability produced sporadic thunderstorm wind damage and small hail in the Lower Susquehanna Valley and in portions of the Middle Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated at 60 mph knocked down a large tree onto a car parked on North Water Street in Selinsgrove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through Northwest Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of December 5th, accompanied by a band of moderate to heavy snow showers and gusty winds up to 40 MPH. Lake effect snow quickly developed into the morning of the 5th as winds shifted more northwesterly and temperatures fell through the 20s behind this front. An initial burst of lake effect snow occurred during the early to mid-morning hours on the 5th, with rates up to 1 inch per hour and 35 to 45 MPH wind gusts common, with up to 55 MPH near Lake Erie. This led to significant impacts to the morning commute, with many local school districts and businesses closing for the day and whiteout conditions and drifting snow reported. After a lull, lake effect snow turned moderate to heavy again during the afternoon and evening hours, leading to impacts to the evening commute. The lake effect snow gradually became less intense and more disorganized by early in the morning on December 6th as winds also subsided significantly. The peak snowfall from this event was 29 inches, measured by a trained spotter in Union City. A CoCoRaHS observer in Corry measured 26.3 inches. Selected spotter reports include 20.7 inches in Venango Township, 17.9 inches in Edinboro, 17.8 inches in Cochranton, and 16.4 inches in Meadville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through Northwest Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of December 5th, accompanied by a band of moderate to heavy snow showers and gusty winds up to 40 MPH. Lake effect snow quickly developed into the morning of the 5th as winds shifted more northwesterly and temperatures fell into the 20s behind this front. The main window of impactful snow and blowing snow occurred through the mid-morning hours on the 5th with significant impacts to the morning commute. 30 to 40 MPH wind gusts combined with moderate to heavy falling snow and tumbling temperatures lead to a period of near-blizzard conditions with very low visibility and extensive drifting snow, leading to very difficult travel conditions. Many local schools and businesses closed for the day, and numerous reports, pictures, and videos from local media, the public, and storm chasers showed whiteout conditions and snow-covered roads, with accidents and snowbound vehicles reported, especially across northern portions of the county . After a relative lull during the late morning and into the afternoon hours, additional lake effect snow bands developed late in the afternoon, persisting into the nighttime hours. Snow gradually lost organization and intensity through the early morning hours of the 6th, with snow showers producing minimal accumulation continuing through the 6th. On top of the very harsh travel conditions during the morning hours of December 5th, some additional roof collapses were noted as new snow and strong winds combined with a heavy pre-existing snowpack. Selected reports from trained spotters include 29 inches in Union City and 17.9 inches in Edinboro, with a CoCoRaHS observer in Corry measuring 26.3 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front blasted through Northwest Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of December 5th, accompanied by a band of moderate to heavy snow showers and gusty winds up to 40 MPH. Lake effect snow quickly developed into the morning of the 5th as winds shifted more northwesterly and temperatures fell into the 20s behind this front. The initial window of impactful snow and blowing snow occurred through the mid-morning hours on the 5th with significant impacts to the morning commute. 30 to 40 MPH wind gusts combined with moderate to heavy falling snow and tumbling temperatures to lead to significantly reduced visibility and very difficult travel conditions. Many local schools closed for the day. After a relative lull during the late morning and into the afternoon hours, additional lake effect snow bands developed late in the afternoon, persisting into the nighttime hours. Snow gradually lost organization and intensity through the early morning hours of the 6th, with snow showers producing generally light accumulation continuing through the 6th. Selected reports from trained spotters include 20.7 inches in Venango Township, 17.8 inches in Cochranton and 16.4 inches near Meadville. A Cooperative Observer in Titusville measured 14.8 inches of snow, with a CoCoRaHS observer in Conneaut Lake reporting 11.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level shortwave provided the focus for convection to develop across northern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of June 20, 2024. High PWAT values near 2 resulted in locally heavy rainfall beneath these slow-moving storms. A report of 2.50 of rainfall in only 25 minutes contributed to localized flash flooding along the headwaters of Pine Creek in Potter County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the vicinity of the headwaters of Pine Creek near Cutler Summit and the village of Brookland in Potter County during the afternoon of June 20, 2024, following 2.50 of rain in only 25 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered convection during the hot and humid afternoon of June 19, 2024 produced some strong thunderstorms across northern Pennsylvania. These storms produced gusty winds and sub-severe hail throughout the afternoon and early evening hours in McKean County before tapering off after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm produced nickel-sized hail between Kane and Lantz Corners, covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and storms developed ahead of an approaching cold front on June 26, 2024. Some of these storms became severe, producing sporadic wind damage and a few severe hail reports across central Pennsylvania. There was also some flash flooding reported across the northwest mountains in portions of Warren and McKean counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Mount Jewett.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface warm front stationed south in northern Maryland began moving northward into central Pennsylvania during the afternoon of June 29, 2024 and continued progressing north through the evening hours. Ample instability coupled with very favorable low-level shear parameters allowed for severe thunderstorms along and ahead of the warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a downburst with winds estimated at 95 mph in Millersburg with damage extending from Millersburg Area High School with damage continuing towards Landfill Road on the east side of Millersburg. The press box at the school's athletic field was blown off supporting metal beams and damaged along with a small ticket office on the other side of the field. Sporadic wind damage continued to be observed along and east of State Route 25 with multiple instances of snapped and uprooted trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting northward from Maryland with increased moisture from the Chesapeake Bay led to strong-to-severe thunderstorms across Lancaster and York counties in southern Pennsylvania. A severe thunderstorm tracking from Maryland into southern York County produced isolated wind damage, weakening further north as it become removed from the best lift and moisture.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and a fence southeast of Stewartstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak surface troughing in an environment of ample instability and moisture allowed for convection to occur across east-central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of June 22, 2024. Severe thunderstorms produced concentrated areas of thunderstorm wind damage with occasional small hail reports throughout the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Hughesville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey confirmed that a microburst occurred near the town of Milesburg as a thunderstorm moved through the area. Maximum wind gusts were estimated at 105 mph. The microburst began along Moose Run Road where numerous trees were snapped and uprooted. Additional trees were uprooted along Johnson Hill Road. A barn and an outbuilding also sustained considerable roof damage as a result of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on July 2, 2024, severe (D2) drought conditions developed across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. The first spot to see D2 conditions was southern Franklin County. The D2 area expanded westward by July 9, 2024 to include southern portions of Fulton, Bedford and Somerset counties, and continued to expand west-northward by July 16, 2024 to encompass much of Fulton, Bedford, and Somerset counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions developed in south-central Franklin County on July 2, 2024 and continued through the end of July.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions developed in southern Fulton County on July 9, 2024 and continued through the end of July.

A cold front dropped southward across Central Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of April 14, 2024, accompanied by a line of showers and storms. The storms developed in a relatively dry environment, with plentiful shear and adequate CAPE. Damaging winds were the primary issue, although there were two reports of quarter-sized hail. The storms weakened as they moved into southern Pennsylvania late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced quarter-sized hail in Brockport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on July 2, 2024, severe (D2) drought conditions developed across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. The first spot to see D2 conditions was southern Franklin County. The D2 area expanded westward by July 9, 2024 to include southern portions of Fulton, Bedford and Somerset counties, and continued to expand west-northward by July 16, 2024 to encompass much of Fulton, Bedford, and Somerset counties, and a portion of far southern Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions developed in southern Bedford County on July 9, 2024 and continued through the end of July, gradually expanding to include most of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front followed by a significant early spring storm system led to a prolonged period of wet weather from April 1 into April 3, 2024. Central Pennsylvania received 2-4 (locally 5+) of rain falling over roughly a 72-hour period, which led to flooding of creeks and rivers in some areas. Additionally, WFO CTP received a call from WFO PHI that a dam on Octoraro Creek in western Chester County was performing an emergency release of water, and there was concern of a bridge or multiple bridges below the dam becoming submerged. This prompted a flood warning for Octoraro Creek below the dam in eastern Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days of rain led to flooded roads and landslides in parts of Cambria County on April 3, 2024. Some impacts include: a landslide that pushed mud and debris across a rail line in Gallitzin Borough near Portage and Spruce Streets; the closure of PA-53 in Lilly Borough due to a shoulder being washed out; the closure of Menoher Blvd in the city of Johnstown due to a mudslide that washed away a portion of the shoulder and also took down high tension power lines; the closure of PA-403 at the intersection of PA-984 in Upper Yoder Township and also just over the Somerset County line near Soap Hollow Road and Kring Street in Tire Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front followed by a significant early spring storm system led to a prolonged period of wet weather from April 1 into April 3, 2024. Central Pennsylvania received 2-4 (locally 5+) of rain falling over roughly a 72-hour period, which led to flooding of creeks and rivers in some areas. Additionally, WFO CTP received a call from WFO PHI that a dam on Octoraro Creek in western Chester County was performing an emergency release of water, and there was concern of a bridge or multiple bridges below the dam becoming submerged. This prompted a flood warning for Octoraro Creek below the dam in eastern Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WFO CTP received notification from WFO PHI that Pine Grove Dam on Octoraro Lake was performing an emergency release of water due to several days of heavy rainfall. Established policy is for the Pine Grove Dam operator to contact WFO PHI, and for WHO PHI to contact WFO CTP with this information. When this type of emergency release is performed, the bridge at Brown Road that crosses Octoraro Creek is at risk of flooding. Therefore, policy is for both WFO CTP and WFO PHI to issue a localized flood warning for the area around Octoraro Creek below Pine Grove Dam.

Additionally, WFO CTP received a call from WFO PHI that a dam on Octoraro Creek in western Chester County was performing an emergency release of water, and there was concern of a bridge or multiple bridges below the dam becoming submerged. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several consecutive days of rain led to a handful of flooded roads in parts of Centre County on April 3, 2024. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on July 2, 2024, severe (D2) drought conditions developed across portions of south-central Pennsylvania. The first spot to see D2 conditions was southern Franklin County. The D2 area expanded westward by July 9, 2024 to include southern portions of Fulton, Bedford and Somerset counties, and continued to expand west-northward by July 16, 2024 to encompass much of Fulton, Bedford, and Somerset counties, and a portion of far southern Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions developed in southern Somerset County on July 9, 2024 and continued through the end of July, gradually expanding to include all of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front approaching central Pennsylvania in an environment of sufficient instability and shear allowed for thunderstorm formation during the late afternoon and evening hours, with multiple rounds of severe thunderstorms crossing the northern tier of Pennsylvania. The multiple rounds of heavy rainfall, coupled with local topography, allowed for flash flooding across hydrologically sensitive areas of Potter and Tioga counties. As thunderstorms progressed southward into the Lower Susquehanna Valley, they began to congeal into a line of thunderstorms producing more widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced lightning that struck a utility pole, resulting in a fire.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees at the intersection of North Brookland Road and Sweden Hill Road in Brookland.

As thunderstorms progressed southward into the Lower Susquehanna Valley, they began to congeal into a line of thunderstorms producing more widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph knocked down multiple trees across US Route 6, blocking one lane of traffic near West Pike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front drifted southeastward from central Lake Huron, southern Lower MI, and northwestern IN to southern ON, southeastern Lower MI, far-northwestern OH, and northern IN during the late morning through early evening of the 26th. The front was preceded by a warm and humid sector comprised of weak to moderate MUCAPE and DCAPE, respectively, and moderate to strong effective bulk shear in northwest PA. Scattered to numerous multicell thunderstorms developed ahead of the front in northwest PA, where isolated small hail and straight-line convective wind damage were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to penny-size about three-miles east of Union City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, a low moved northeastward from Georgian Bay to southwestern QC during the late morning through early evening of the 24th. Simultaneously, the trailing cold front swept southeastward from far-southwestern ON, far-western Lake Erie, and northwest OH to eastern Lake Erie, northwest PA, and northeast OH. This front encountered a warm and humid sector along the northwestern flank of the Bermuda-Azores subtropical high pressure ridge.  Ahead of the front, a cluster of multicell thunderstorms impacted the Erie metro area and generated isolated, damaging straight-line convective wind gusts. These storms occurred in an environment comprised of moderate effective bulk shear, MUCAPE, and DCAPE, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs downed along Dynes Avenue in Erie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beginning on July 30, 2024, extreme (D3) drought conditions developed across portions of south-central Pennsylvania, including southern Somerset County, southern Bedford County, and far southwestern Fulton County. Additionally, D2 conditions developed across far southern Cambria County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D3 (extreme) drought conditions developed in far southwestern Fulton County on July 30, 2024 and continued through the end of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the surface, a wavy warm front swept eastward from Lake Michigan, southern Lower MI, and western OH to southern Lake Huron, southwestern ON, eastern Lake Erie, and northwest PA as an occluded low drifted from central Lake Superior to central ON during the mid-morning through early afternoon hours of the 29th. Multiple lines of multicell showers and thunderstorms developed and persisted generally eastward along and ahead of the surface warm front in northwest PA during the early afternoon. These storms occurred amidst weak to moderate MUCAPE and DCAPE, respectively, and moderate to strong effective bulk shear. Isolated straight-line convective wind damage occurred in the Meadville area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed in Vernon Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front in an environment favorable for severe weather with sufficient instability, plenty of moisture, and wind shear. This supported the development of some strong to severe storms in the afternoon and early evening. Damaging wind gusts knocked down numerous trees and wires in Susquehanna County and there was one microburst surveyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees onto a house which resulted in some damage to the gutters along the roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough over the Great Lakes interacting with a quasi-stationary boundary over eastern Ohio and western PA spawned severe weather from mid afternoon through late evening. MLCAPE near 2000 J/kg along with a relatively strong, elongated shear profiles led to an environment for strong to severe winds and possible tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid day with increasing upper level heights kept the area largely dry, however, isolated convection was able to kick off  along a few old outflow boundaries from the previous day, with just enough dry air aloft to potentially see damaging wind gusts with stronger updrafts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Hopper Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance in the upper atmosphere slowly moved across the northern tier of Pennsylvania during the afternoon. This feature, along with a stationary frontal boundary at the surface over Northeast Pennsylvania triggered scattered to numerous thunderstorms during the heat of the day. These storms moved very slowly over the region producing torrential rainfall and causing localized flash flooding in Luzerne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water over roads in West Nanticoke with several streets closed to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwesterly wind ahead of an approaching front eventually advected sufficient moisture and warm air in the lower levels to overcome a capping inversion ahead of a cold front that would eventually be the  main driver of forcing to take advantage of a supportive environment. Damaging wind was the primary threat, although the shear and lapse rates aloft pointed towards a limited potential for large hail as well. Tornadoes looked possible, however, LCLs were relatively high, and any issued TORs did not verify.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front tracking across Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours on October 6, 2024 in an environment with strong shear and sufficient instability allowed for thunderstorms to form in western Pennsylvania. As these storms moved into central Pennsylvania, they encountered a more stable air mass with less shear but marginally severe thunderstorms continued into portions of Warren and Elk Counties, producing isolated thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60mph knocked down trees and wires onto Route 337 Road south of Tidioute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture had spread over Northeast Pennsylvania ahead of the remnants of Tropical Storm Debby. Prolonged torrential rains blanketed the region during the night and early morning hours before becoming enhanced by the approaching tropical remnants. This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. Many swiftwater rescue teams were deployed to extricate stranded residents from their homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An occupied vehicle was reported to be stranded in the roadway due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front in an environment favorable for severe weather with sufficient instability, plenty of moisture, and wind shear. This supported the development of some strong to severe storms in the afternoon and early evening. Damaging wind gusts knocked down numerous trees and wires in Susquehanna County and there was one microburst surveyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough over the Great Lakes interacting with a quasi-stationary boundary over eastern Ohio and western PA spawned severe weather from mid afternoon through late evening. MLCAPE near 2000 J/kg along with a relatively strong, elongated shear profiles led to an environment for strong to severe winds and possible tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage to the Mercer County Courthouse clock was reported by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwesterly wind ahead of an approaching front eventually advected sufficient moisture and warm air in the lower levels to overcome a capping inversion ahead of a cold front that would eventually be the  main driver of forcing to take advantage of a supportive environment. Damaging wind was the primary threat, although the shear and lapse rates aloft pointed towards a limited potential for large hail as well. Tornadoes looked possible, however, LCLs were relatively high, and any issued TORs did not verify.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along Valencia Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture had spread over Northeast Pennsylvania ahead of the remnants of Tropical Storm Debby. Prolonged torrential rains blanketed the region during the night and early morning hours before becoming enhanced by the approaching tropical remnants. This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. Many swiftwater rescue teams were deployed to extricate stranded residents from their homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding from Snake Creek damaged and closed Bailey Road south of the intersection with State Route 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper low over northern Lake Michigan shifted east while the the approach of a pre-frontal trough and destabilization resulted in convective development along and ahead of a cold frontal boundary. Colder and drier mid-levels allowed for MLCAPE  values near 1000-2000 J/kg and mid-level lapse rates of 6-6.5 C/km. Effective shear was marginal, with values between 20 and 30 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a wire in the backyard of a house.

Many swiftwater rescue teams were deployed to extricate stranded residents from their homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many streets and roads were flooded in and around Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with an approaching cold front and a favorable position of the jet stream triggered numerous heavy rain producing thunderstorms over northeastern Pennsylvania. Locally heavy rainfall totaled between 3 and 4 inches over a short period of time which caused areas of significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creamery Road flooded and closed to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front in an environment favorable for severe weather with sufficient instability, plenty of moisture, and wind shear. This supported the development of some strong to severe storms in the afternoon and early evening. Damaging wind gusts knocked down numerous trees and wires in Susquehanna County and there was one microburst surveyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm descended southeast into Susquehanna County around 5 pm on August 5th. The intense core of the storm collapsed over Northeast Susquehanna County resulting in damaging microburst winds at the surface over a wide area. A storm survey indicated spotty tree damage along Mill Road but increased in amount of snapped and uprooted trees the farther west down Mill Road. The worst damage found was just north of Mill Road before the road began to rise sharply on the edge of a swamp. There were dozens of uprooted trees and snapped limbs with winds estimated near 95 mph at this location. The damage continued South of Mill Road up the hill side with many uprooted trees with wind estimates of 80 to 90 mph to the crest of the hill. At the top of the hill, there was more chaotic damage due to topographical effects. The pattern of tree fall for the remainder of the downburst was consistent with strong straight line winds with wind direction channeling along valleys and hillsides.

A slow-moving upper low over northern Lake Michigan shifted east while the the approach of a pre-frontal trough and destabilization resulted in convective development along and ahead of a cold frontal boundary. Colder and drier mid-levels allowed for MLCAPE  values near 1000-2000 J/kg and mid-level lapse rates of 6-6.5 C/km. Effective shear was marginal, with values between 20 and 30 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A departing low pressure system moving off the eastern seaboard of the United States allowed for an extended period of northwesterly flow across central Pennsylvania, allowing for lake effect snow showers to drift into northwestern Pennsylvania from the evening hours of December 2, 2024 and continuing through the morning hours of December 4th, when a surface trough began to disrupt northwesterly flow and shut off the prolonged lake effect snow. The heaviest snowfall by the end of the event was confined to northwestern Warren County, with western portions of McKean County also receiving upwards of 8-10 inches of snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow bands produced 8 to 12 inches of snow across Warren County with amounts as high as 16 inches near Russell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching area of surface low pressure alongside a negatively tilted shortwave trough, allowing some thunderstorms to form across the southern tier of Pennsylvania. Marginally severe thunderstorms formed across Somerset County during the late afternoon hours of December 31, 2024, producing multiple instances of tree damage along with producing structural damage to a barn in Somerset. Stable air farther to the north and east of Somerset County limited thunderstorm potential, especially after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing wind gusts estimated near 60 mph knocked down multiple trees in Rockwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began during the early morning hours of December 5, 2024 across the northern tier with warm air advection within an enhanced low level jet ahead of a cold frontal passage beginning to overspread snowfall across central Pennsylvania. Enhanced frontogenetic forcing and instability in the lower levels allowed for some snow squalls to form across the Laurel Highlands and Middle Susquehanna Valley, with some snow bands continuing into the Lower Susquehanna during the morning commute. Northwesterly flow behind the cold frontal passage allowed for lingering lake effect snow showers across portions of northwestern Pennsylvania, with heavy lake effect snow continuing through December 6th. A snow squall along Interstate 80 in southern Clinton County caused a multi-vehicle accident with 10 tractor trailers and 3 to 4 passenger vehicles, closing the roadway for multiple hours and injuring two motorists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. An RWIS site on Interstate 99 near Port Matilda reported a 2 to 3 degree drop in surface temperatures along with visibility dropping to one quarter mile within a snow squall. Webcams in the surrounding area also showed accumulating snow on roadways in the squall for a period of 5 to 10 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. RWIS data indicated a rapid drop in visibility to a quarter of a mile with roadways becoming snow covered quickly in a snow squall. Air and surface temperatures were already below freezing before the snow squall, but data indicated a 4 degree drop in surface temperatures during the squall. This squall resulted in a multi-vehicle incident on Interstate 80 involving 10 tractor trailers and 3 to 4 passenger vehicles, closing the roadway for multiple hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the early afternoon of December 4th, and continued into the evening. This snow was generally light, with snowfall totals under 2 inches reported across Warren County. After a break in the snowfall, lake effect snow bands began to move in from the northwest. This snow continued through early afternoon on Friday, December 5th. While most of the county saw 4 to 6 inches of snow from these lake effect bands, 8 inches of snow fell in the city of Warren and 9 inches fell in Tidioute.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from around 3-4. 4.0 was reported near Mount Pocono, Delaware Water Gap, and Canadensis. Additionally, a light glaze of freezing rain occurred, with 0.02 reported at the MPO airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall began during the early morning hours of December 5, 2024 across the northern tier with warm air advection within an enhanced low level jet ahead of a cold frontal passage beginning to overspread snowfall across central Pennsylvania. Enhanced frontogenetic forcing and instability in the lower levels allowed for some snow squalls to form across the Laurel Highlands and Middle Susquehanna Valley, with some snow bands continuing into the Lower Susquehanna during the morning commute. Northwesterly flow behind the cold frontal passage allowed for lingering lake effect snow showers across portions of northwestern Pennsylvania, with heavy lake effect snow continuing through December 6th. A snow squall along Interstate 80 in southern Clinton County caused a multi-vehicle accident with 10 tractor trailers and 3 to 4 passenger vehicles, closing the roadway for multiple hours and injuring two motorists.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the early afternoon of December 4th, and continued into the evening. This snow was generally light, with snowfall totals under 2 inches reported across Elk County. After a break in the snowfall overnight, lake effect snow began to fall during the early morning hours of Thursday, December 5th. The snow continued to fall periodically through the early afternoon on Friday, December 6th. Snow totals across much of Elk County ranged from 2 to 4 inches, though 6.5 inches of snow was reported near the town of Force.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer than average temperatures during the previous three months along with insufficient rainfall, much below average streamflows, groundwater levels and dried out soil conditions continued Severe Drought conditions in parts of Northeastern PA. Conditions lingered through the 17th in Lackawanna, Pike and Wayne counties when sufficient precipitation allowed for a reduction in drought severity back to just abnormally dry conditions. Severe drought continued through the 31st for a small portion of southern Luzerne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared Severe Drought over all of the county as a result of insufficient rainfall during the past 90 days coupled with low streamflows, groundwater levels and drier than average soil moisture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures were observed across portions of southwestern Pennsylvania, beginning on December 22, 2024 and continuing into the afternoon hours of December 23. A 75-year-old woman fell outside of her home in Cambria County on December 22nd and was discovered hours later before being rushed to a local medical center and later succumbing to hypothermia on December 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold temperatures, with wind chills ranging from zero to 10 degrees, were observed across Cambria County throughout the daytime hours on December 22, 2024 and continued until the afternoon hours of December 23. A 75-year-old woman fell outside of her home on December 22nd and was discovered hours later before being rushed to a local medical center and later succumbing to hypothermia on December 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously cold air moving in northwesterly flow over the still relatively warm Great Lakes resulted in a time period of well overperforming convective snow bands over Beaver, Allegheny, and parts of Butler county.  Snow amounts in these areas ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts across northern parts of the Pittsburgh metro area. An Winter Weather Advisory was issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulation of 5.5 inches was reported in near Freedom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions began across southeastern Pennsylvania in early November 2024, including portions of Lancaster, Lebanon, Schuylkill, Columbia, and Northumberland counties. Dry conditions persisting throughout the end of the month allowed for severe drought conditions to continue through the end of November 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions began across Lancaster County in early November 2024 before continuing dry conditions allowed further expansion to include most of the county by the end of November 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep upper level low pressure moved into the western Great Lakes on November 20th. A strong shortwave rotating around this upper low across Ohio helped to drive a cold front across the Upper Ohio Valley during the late afternoon hours. A thin line of showers accompanied the front, with little to no lightning. The front worked with sufficient instability aloft to bring strong wind gusts to the surface, with several in the 45 to 60 MPH range according to various observations. These gusts were responsible for the many reports of wind damage received across southwest Pennsylvania. Strong low-level shear and weakly veered wind profiles also supported a brief tornado on the north side of Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of trees and wires down were received in Butler County.  A few areas that were impacted were Forward Township, Center Township, Butler Township, Penn Township, and Oakland Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching area of surface low pressure with an associated warm and cold front allowed for some areas of instability across southern portions of the Laurel Highlands and south-central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening hours of November 20, 2024. This allowed for gusty showers and an occasional thunderstorm that produced isolated instances of tree and wire damage across Cambria County. Winds ahead of and behind the cold front allowed for trees and wires to fall in portions of Somerset County, where one tree fell on a roadway and a driver drove into a tree, resulting in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds, estimated near 45 mph, knocked down a tree onto Cumberland Highway near the intersection of Palo Alto Road in Southampton Township. Rainfall close to sunset in an area with no streetlights led to a vehicle running into the tree, killing the driver of the vehicle.  There were no other fatalities or injuries from this incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions over a span of many weeks led to the most active fall fire season across central Pennsylvania, with 585 fire incidents between September and November, burning just under 2000 acres in this timeframe. The most significant wildfire developed during the afternoon of November 8, 2024 in Frankstown Township near Canoe Creek State Park in Blair County in an extended period of dry and unseasonably warm temperatures, with gusty winds. The wildfire grew rapidly to encompass 300 acres. At the fire's peak, aerial support was enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Winds began to decrease during the late morning hours of November 10th with scattered rain showers assisting firefighters in putting out the blaze by the late afternoon and evening hours. While attempting to put the wildfire out, three firefighters sustained minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire developed during the afternoon of November 8, 2024 in Frankstown Township near Canoe Creek State Park in Blair County. The wildfire occurred during a stretch of unseasonably warm and dry weather along with gusty winds, and it quickly expanded in size to around 300 acres. At the fire's peak, aerial support was enlisted to battle the fire, along with firefighting crews on the ground. Winds began to decrease during the late morning hours of November 10th with scattered rain showers assisting firefighters in putting out the blaze by the late afternoon and evening hours. While attempting to put the wildfire out, three firefighters sustained minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued into the beginning stages of December 2024 across portions of southwestern Somerset County. Beneficial snowfall allowed for improvement across the area, with drought status dropping below severe criteria during the first week of December.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions continued across southwestern Somerset County during the early stages of December 2024, with continued snowfall allowing for severe drought allowed for relief across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage during the early morning hours of December 12, 2024 provided ample lift in an environment with surface-based instability to spawn snow squalls along Interstate 80 in Clearfield County. Road surface temperatures slightly above freezing prior to the snow squall allowed for flash freeze potential with heavy snow covering roadways in a 5 to 10 minute span prior to sunrise. These snow squalls closed the westbound lane of Interstate 80 between exits 120 and 133 for an extended period of time with multiple incidents with five injuries with no fatalities reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. An RWIS sensor on at exit 120 on Interstate 80 indicated a rapid drop in visibility to a quarter of a mile in heavy snow. Road surface temperatures dropped from 34 degrees to 28 degrees during the snow squall. This snow squall resulted in multiple crashes on the westbound lane of Interstate 80 due to snow-covered and icy roadways. These accidents resulted in five injuries with no fatalities reported as a result of the snow squall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. An RWIS sensor at mile marker 146 of Interstate 80 observed a rapid drop to one-quarter mile visibility in a snow squall. Heavy snowfall with this snow squall covered the highway with road temps already below freezing at the time of the snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass brought ripe conditions for the development of thunderstorms as a weak front moved in. Thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts bringing downed trees and wires to portions of eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down onto a mobile home on Fawn Grove Lane. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass was in place as a cold front moved in. This resulted in several strong to severe thunderstorms developing, with storms mainly bringing damaging wind gusts to portions of eastern Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey conducted on wind damage in Kidder Township near Lake Harmony. Path of snapped and uprooted trees began on Chalet Court. Along Chalet Court and Lupine Drive from Chalet to Spring Street snapped and uprooted trees caused damage to nearby residences including damage to sheds and porch railings. Near the intersection of Lupine Drive and Spring Street, at least 10 trees were either uprooted or snapped in a wooded lot. Additional trees were uprooted along Pineknoll Drive. More sporadic tree damage, including large limbs felled, was noted along the loop of Skye Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough digging across the Upper Midwest and into the Great Lakes region associated with a strong surface low crossing Ontario resulted in widespread, synoptic snowfall across the region. Snow ratios rapidly increased, yielding a transition from wet, heavier snow to lighter powder.  The highest reported amounts were in northwestern PA, where between 6 and 12 inches fell along and north of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported 8 inches of snow in Jamestown, PA.

The highest reported amounts were in northwestern PA, where between 6 and 12 inches fell along and north of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 9.7 inches of snow measured near Marienville, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions began across eastern York County mid-November 2024 and continued through the end of November 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions began across eastern York County on November 19, 2024 with dry conditions continuing through the end of November 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure across New England began slowly moving westward during the late evening hours of November 21 and into November 22, 2024 allowing for a long-duration snow event for portions of central Pennsylvania. A strong pressure gradient northwest of the area of low pressure also allowed for gusty winds throughout the event with a wet snow prompting multiple trees and wires down throughout portions of Sullivan County, resulting in over 70% of the county having no power. There were also multiple roads closed across portions of Cambria and Somerset Counties with disabled vehicles, trees/wires down, and jack-knifed trailers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 6 to 10 inches of snow across Cambria County with higher elevations seeing up to 1 foot of snow from the early morning through the late evening of November 22, 2024. State Route 553 and US Route 22 West were closed for a period of time, with multiple accidents reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalously cold air moving in northwesterly flow over the still relatively warm Great Lakes resulted in a time period of well overperforming convective snow bands over Beaver, Allegheny, and parts of Butler county.  Snow amounts in these areas ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts across northern parts of the Pittsburgh metro area. An Winter Weather Advisory was issued.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 6 inches of accumulation in Baden, PA in Beaver county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued through the month of November 2024 across portions of southwestern Pennsylvania, including portions of Somerset County, far southern Cambria County, and southwestern Bedford County. Beneficial rainfall and snowfall across Cambria and Bedford counties allowed for improvement across the area late in the month, with drought status dropping below severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 (severe) drought conditions continued across much of Somerset County throughout the first half of November 2024 before beneficial rainfall and snowfall allowed for some recovery across eastern portions of the county. D2 (severe) drought conditions continues across southwestern portions of Somerset County throughout the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced heavy snow across Somerset County from the early morning of November 22, 2024 through the late evening. Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches with higher elevations seeing 14 to 18 inches of snow. A jackknifed trailer caused US-219 north to be closed for a period of time. PA-403 was also closed near Conemaugh Township  due to downed trees and wires.

There were also multiple roads closed across portions of Cambria and Somerset Counties with disabled vehicles, trees/wires down, and jack-knifed trailers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced 6 to 8 inches of snow across Sullivan County with higher elevations receiving 13 to 15 inches of snow from the evening of November 21 through the early afternoon hours of November 22, 2024. Heavy, wet snow coupled with gusty winds knocked down multiple trees and wires across the county, resulting in approximately 70% of the county being without power during the event. State Routes 487 and 87 were both closed for a period of time due to trees and wires being knocked down on the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep upper level low pressure moved into the western Great Lakes on November 20th. A strong shortwave rotating around this upper low across Ohio helped to drive a cold front across the Upper Ohio Valley during the late afternoon hours. A thin line of showers accompanied the front, with little to no lightning. The front worked with sufficient instability aloft to bring strong wind gusts to the surface, with several in the 45 to 60 MPH range according to various observations. These gusts were responsible for the many reports of wind damage received across southwest Pennsylvania. Strong low-level shear and weakly veered wind profiles also supported a brief tornado on the north side of Pittsburgh.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A team from the National Weather Service in Pittsburgh identified a coherent swath of likely tornadic damage that began along Stanton Ave in the Highland Park neighborhood of Pittsburgh and continued to along Campania Ave in the Lincoln-Lemington-Belmar area. Damage was sporadic along this path, suggesting the circulation occasionally weakened below tornadic intensity along this track, possibly owing to variability in terrain. The most concentrated damage was observed along Lemington Ave, where a couple trees were down, small and large limbs were snapped, and shingles were removed from homes. There is no evidence that power poles in this area were damaged by wind, which puts an effective upper bound on wind speed estimates here. Debris lofted by the tornado (likely leaves and small twigs) was noted in data from KPBZ radar, which continued in the air downstream for several miles as strong, non-tornadic wind surged eastward into the Penn Hills area. This narrow tornado was embedded within a broader swath of 40-60 mph non-tornadic wind, as evidenced by threshold tree litter. Pending additional information, this tornado will be rated EF0 with maximal wind of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough moving across central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours of November 29, 2024 allowed for snow squalls across western Pennsylvania despite limited instability due to occurring ahead of sunrise. A couple of snow squalls occurred across Clearfield County during the morning hours, with heavy snow and lowered visibility observed on RWIS webcams and sensors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SNOW SQUALL. An RWIS site along Interstate 80 southeast of Penfield reported visibility of less than 1/4 mile in heavy snow with a road surface temperature of 31.4F and air temperature 28F during the snow squall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley during the afternoon and evening hours of September 6th.  Sufficient instability and shear supported a few reports of large hail, but downdraft CAPE values of 1000 to 1200 J/kg were key in the creation of a number of damaging wind gusts with some of the storms. Mainly trees and power poles were affected.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were reported down in the area.

Deep-layer shear supported storm organization and rotation, leading to several instances of damaging wind, and a couple isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on to wires.

Warmer than average temperatures during the previous three months along with insufficient rainfall, much below average streamflows, groundwater levels and dried out soil conditions prompted the development of Severe Drought conditions in parts of Northeastern PA. Conditions reached this drought threshold during the second week of the month and held steady through the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor declared Severe Drought over the southern quarter of the county as a result of insufficient rainfall during the past 90 days coupled with low streamflows, groundwater levels and drier than average soil moisture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snowfall to portions of eastern Pennsylvania and adjacent areas of northwest New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from as low as a dusting to an inch in lower elevations to to as much as 15.3 inches across the Pocono Plateau, as reported near Lake Harmony. Snowfall totals were 6 or greater across the northern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching area of surface low pressure with an associated warm and cold front allowed for some areas of instability across southern portions of the Laurel Highlands and south-central Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and evening hours of November 20, 2024. This allowed for gusty showers and an occasional thunderstorm that produced isolated instances of tree and wire damage across Cambria County. Winds ahead of and behind the cold front allowed for trees and wires to fall in portions of Somerset County, where one tree fell on a roadway and a driver drove into a tree, resulting in a fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires onto William Penn Avenue near the intersection of Varner Lane south of Vinco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that moved off the coast early on Thursday, the 21st, retrograded back to the north and west across the Poconos and into the Twin Tiers Thursday night into early Friday, the 22nd.  It tapped into moisture from both the Atlantic and off of Lake Ontario. Widespread rain showers spread from east to west as the low moved inland, and as colder air was drawn in from the northwest out of Canada, rain mixed with and changed over to snow especially late in the afternoon and evening on the 21st starting in the higher terrain and working down toward the valleys. Periods of snow continued overnight with some localized heavy banding across portions of Northeast PA where snowfall rates ranged from 1-3 inches per hour. The heavy, wet nature of the snow given the marginal temperatures in place, led to some tree branch damage due to the weight of the snow which led to scattered to widespread power outages. As drier air moved in early on the 22nd, snow tapered off to some lingering rain and snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure that moved off the coast early on Thursday, the 21st, retrograded back to the north and west across the Poconos and into the Twin Tiers Thursday night into early Friday, the 22nd.  It tapped into moisture from both the Atlantic and off of Lake Ontario. Widespread rain showers spread from east to west as the low moved inland, and as colder air was drawn in from the northwest out of Canada, rain mixed with and changed over to snow especially late in the afternoon and evening on the 21st starting in the higher terrain and working down toward the valleys. Periods of snow continued overnight with some localized heavy banding across portions of Northeast PA where snowfall rates ranged from 1-3 inches per hour. The heavy, wet nature of the snow given the marginal temperatures in place, led to some tree branch damage due to the weight of the snow which led to scattered to widespread power outages. As drier air moved in early on the 22nd, snow tapered off to some lingering rain and snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 8 and 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon through early evening of October 6th, a surface cold front moved eastward across NE OH and into NW PA. Along the front, a broken line of multicell thunderstorms and isolated supercell thunderstorms developed and persisted generally eastward in NW PA. These storms produced sporadic straight-line convective wind damage and severe hail amidst moderate to strong effective bulk shear, weak to moderate MUCAPE, and moderate DCAPE in the warm and relatively-moist sector.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed in Titusville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of tropical moisture had spread over Northeast Pennsylvania ahead of the remnants of Tropical Storm Debby. Prolonged torrential rains blanketed the region during the night and early morning hours before becoming enhanced by the approaching tropical remnants. This enhancement of rainfall led to widespread flash flooding in many parts of the region where roads and bridges were destroyed and washed away along with numerous impacted properties. Many swiftwater rescue teams were deployed to extricate stranded residents from their homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous homes and businesses were flooded including the Thompson Hose Company firehouse.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed and roof blown-off house along PA Route 27, between Townline Road and North Wayland Road, on the east side of Meadville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed a swath of light to moderate showers across the Upper Ohio Valley during the morning and midday hours of May 11th. Behind this boundary, the increase in low-level moisture and partial clearing helped instability to recover fairly rapidly, and allowed for an increase in shear as well. Discrete storms then fired ahead of an advancing cold front during the early afternoon, and then they advanced across southwest Pennsylvania and northern West Virginia through the remainder of the afternoon and early evening. In eastern Ohio, all storm reports were of hail between 0.75 and 1.25 inch diameter, but as storms matured in southwest Pennsylvania, hail reports increased up to 1.75 inch, as well as a few reports of straight-line wind. Storms closer to the PA/WV border were able to take advantage of sufficient low-level instability and surface vorticity to allow for the development of three tornadoes, one of which was a more significant EF2 twister.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms around slowly-moving Middle Ohio Valley low pressure created isolated flooding concerns in Allegheny County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closures were reported on McMillan, Morrow, and McLaughlin Run Roads due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave trough and an advancing cold front worked with ample levels of instability and shear to initiate thunderstorms over eastern Ohio around midday on May 25th.  The storms increased in coverage and intensity in southwest Pennsylvania through the afternoon and early evening, and continued into the early morning hours of May 26th south of the PA/WV border. Numerous reports of tree and power line damage were received in southwest Pennsylvania, along with reports of hail up to 2 inches in diameter.  In addition, a brief and weak tornado occurred near Raccoon Creek State Park in Beaver County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Cedar Run Road, trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few weak disturbances tracked along the northern periphery of a strong upper ridge, triggering scattered thunderstorms. Many of the storms developed near a frontal boundary that was approaching the New York-Pennsylvania border. With heat, humidity and instability in place, some thunderstorms moved very slowly producing 1 to 2 inches of rainfall within an hour which contributed to flooding in the metropolitan areas surrounding Wilkes-Barre.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets and roads were flooded in West Pittston. Several vehicles were stranded in flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This day, May 17th, will be remembered as one of the more unusual tornado days in southwest Pennsylvania. Although overall levels of instability were fairly low, enough of that parameter, plus shear, was concentrated in the 0 to 3 km layer to allow for the formation of low-topped supercells during the early to mid-afternoon hours. Interaction with a surface boundary allowed for tornadogenesis in a few of the storms as low-level streamwise vorticity was ingested into updrafts. Three of the tornadoes were fairly weak, at EF0 level, with one EF1 impacting areas within the Pittsburgh city limits, including the Pittsburgh Zoo. No straight-line wind or hail reports were received, as the storms were small and as the environment did not support those impacts. One isolated flood report was also received in Allegheny County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video evidence confirmed a brief, weak tornado tracking just north of the Allegheny County Airport. Video analysis puts the track across the industrial complex located at Noble Drive. No known damage was observed, so wind estimates are unknown.

Interaction with a surface boundary allowed for tornadogenesis in a few of the storms as low-level streamwise vorticity was ingested into updrafts. Three of the tornadoes were fairly weak, at EF0 level, with one EF1 impacting areas within the Pittsburgh city limits, including the Pittsburgh Zoo. No straight-line wind or hail reports were received, as the storms were small and as the environment did not support those impacts. One isolated flood report was also received in Allegheny County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was first noted in the vicinity of the Penn Township  Municipal Complex, where flag poles were bent, and a tree limb was snapped. The damage swath continued sporadically over the remainder of the track to Greensburg-Jeannette Regional Airport.  Although damage throughout was primarily to trees, some impact was observed in a neighborhood along Seslar Drive, where a few homes |suffered minor removal of shingles/siding/soffit. At the Regional Airport, a large tree was snapped.||Video was provided from multiple sources of persistent low-level rotation that sporadically contracted and tightened the ground circulation to tornadic strength, leading to the appearance of the tornado skipping over areas. The structural damage and some of the tree damage is consistent |with peak wind gusts of 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing shortwave trough and an advancing cold front worked with ample levels of instability and shear to initiate thunderstorms over eastern Ohio around midday on May 25th.  The storms increased in coverage and intensity in southwest Pennsylvania through the afternoon and early evening, and continued into the early morning hours of May 26th south of the PA/WV border. Numerous reports of tree and power line damage were received in southwest Pennsylvania, along with reports of hail up to 2 inches in diameter.  In addition, a brief and weak tornado occurred near Raccoon Creek State Park in Beaver County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey concluded that a brief tornado touchdown occurred along Pleasant Hills road, just north of the Raccoon Creek State Park boundary in Beaver County, PA. Damage was limited to a utility pole and several mature trees on adjacent properties either side of the road. One medium sized hardwood was uprooted, while a second large, but diseased maple tree was snapped. Several other additional trees had large branches broken off. A peak wind speed of 75 MPH was estimated. It was noted that neither property, nor a shed, garage or RV suffered damage. Additionally, the overwhelming vast majority of other trees and vegetation had no damage. In addition to the low-reflectivity hook echo and velocity couple observed on both KPBZ and TPIT Doppler radars, one of the residents provided video of a slender funnel cloud as the storm passed. Aerial drone footage, a ground survey of the Raccoon Creek State Park, nor interviews with Pennsylvania DCNR rangers were able to confirm any additional damage as the circulation passed through extremely rough and densely forested terrain over the state park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on initial reports of tree damage in the Morningside neighborhood, the track of a tornadic ground circulation began  just outside of Highland Park and continued northeast across the Pittsburgh Zoo and across the Allegheny River, weakening below tornadic intensity as it entered Aspinwall.||Multiple trees were reported down in the Morningside neighborhood and across Highland Park. The circulation intensified as it approached the Pittsburgh Zoo, where several trees were felled and large limbs snapped. A large tree fell on a vehicle in the zoo's parking lot. No injuries were noted at the facility to humans or animals. Based on damage, peak wind during this phase of the tornado was 80 mph.||The circulation strengthened considerably as it descended from Highland Park to the river, with a large swath of hardwood trees felled along Allegheny River Blvd near the Highland Park Bridge. Power poles in this area also were leaning. The swath of damage continued to riverside. Damage here was consistent with peak wind of 105 mph.||Although no damage was observed across the river in Aspinwall, observers in Aspinwall noted a funnel cloud at ground level before dissipating near Waterworks shopping plaza. Additionally, radar data is consistent with the lofting of water into the tornadic circulation, suggesting the ground circulation was still in progress as the storm crossed into Aspinwall.||Because of the storm's proximity to the KPBZ radar, a tornadic debris signature (TDS) was noted in the radar data, indicating the lofting of debris as the tornado moved across Highland Park. Numerous videos provided to NWS Pittsburgh also confirm the mesocyclone structure of the storm/tornado as it advanced across Highland Park.
